name: 1-MNA
description: A methylated niacin metabolite explored for endothelial function, vascular resilience, and healthy aging support.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: 1-Methylnicotinamide is a downstream methylated metabolite of nicotinamide metabolism. It appears to support endothelial signaling, nitric-oxide-related vascular function, and prostacyclin-linked microcirculatory activity while also intersecting with NAD+ pathway turnover and low-grade inflammatory signaling.

bioavailability.notes: Human pharmacokinetic data are limited, but oral use appears practical at low milligram doses. Taking it with food is reasonable if the stomach is sensitive, though it does not appear to require fat for absorption.

half_life.notes: Plasma exposure appears relatively short, while downstream endothelial and metabolic effects may outlast the parent compound.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: No major food interaction is established. Taking it with breakfast or lunch can make tolerability easier for sensitive users.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning or midday dosing is the most practical default when using 1-MNA for vascular or daytime metabolic support.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: There is no established need to cycle 1-MNA, but long-term human outcome data are still limited, so periodic reassessment is sensible.

known_interactions.description: Human interaction data are limited, but because 1-MNA may influence endothelial and prostacyclin-related signaling, extra caution is reasonable alongside medications that increase bleeding risk.

synergies.mechanism: Upstream nicotinamide precursors may complement 1-MNA's downstream role inside nicotinamide metabolism when the overall goal is NAD+ pathway support.
Both may support vascular tone and inflammatory balance through different but potentially complementary pathways.

ai_profile.safety_notes: 1-MNA is promising but still less established than foundational supplements. Use extra caution in pregnancy, breastfeeding, active liver disease, or alongside prescription anticoagulants until stronger human safety data are available.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is based on mechanistic work, preclinical studies, and a smaller human literature focused on vascular, metabolic, and exercise-related endpoints rather than large long-term outcome trials.

name: 5-HTP
description: A serotonin precursor commonly used for sleep onset, mood support, and appetite regulation.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: 5-HTP is the direct metabolic precursor to serotonin and can cross the blood-brain barrier more readily than tryptophan. It is used to raise central serotonin availability, which can influence mood regulation, satiety, and downstream melatonin production involved in sleep timing.

bioavailability.notes: 5-HTP is absorbed reasonably well orally. Taking it away from large protein-heavy meals can be practical when the goal is central serotonin support, but the main limiter is usually tolerability rather than absorption.

half_life.notes: The circulating half-life is fairly short, so timing matters more than it does for longer-acting nutritional compounds.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: A light snack is reasonable if nausea occurs, but avoid combining it with a large protein-heavy meal if the primary goal is sleep support.
timing_recommendations.notes: Evening or bedtime use is the default when the main goal is sleep onset. Morning use is more situational and more likely to cause daytime GI discomfort.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: There is no standard cycling protocol, but periodic reassessment is sensible because serotonergic supplements can lose practicality if they cause nausea, vivid dreams, or medication conflicts.

known_interactions.description: Combining 5-HTP with prescription serotonergic antidepressants can raise serotonin excessively and increase the risk of serotonin toxicity.
MAO inhibitors already raise monoamine levels and should not be combined with 5-HTP without specialist oversight.
Additional serotonergic medications or supplements can compound the risk of agitation, sweating, tremor, and serotonin toxicity.

synergies.mechanism: Magnesium supports relaxation through different neurophysiologic pathways while 5-HTP targets serotonin and melatonin precursors.
Glycine can support sleep temperature regulation and subjective sleep quality while 5-HTP targets serotonergic and melatonin-related sleep signaling.

ai_profile.safety_notes: The main practical risk with 5-HTP is not the supplement itself in isolation but combining it with medications or supplements that already raise serotonin. Start low and avoid self-stacking it with antidepressants, MAO inhibitors, or multiple serotonergic agents.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is mixed but meaningful for sleep and some mood-related use cases, with the biggest real-world limitation being tolerability and medication interaction risk rather than simple inefficacy.

name: AG1
description: A broad-spectrum greens and micronutrient formula meant to cover basic nutritional gaps, digestive support, and general wellness rather than a targeted longevity mechanism.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: AG1 is a composite formula rather than a single active ingredient. Its effect comes from combined micronutrients, plant extracts, probiotics, digestive enzymes, and adaptogenic compounds that may help cover nutritional gaps and modestly support digestion, energy metabolism, and stress resilience. Because it is a proprietary blend, the exact contribution of many actives is difficult to quantify.

bioavailability.notes: Bioavailability varies by nutrient and ingredient. It is best understood as a nutritional convenience product rather than a highly optimized delivery system for any one compound.

half_life.notes: A mixed formula contains vitamins, minerals, probiotics, and botanicals with very different pharmacokinetics, so there is no single meaningful half-life.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Many users take it on an empty stomach in the morning, but taking it with a light meal can improve comfort if it causes nausea or bloating.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning use fits best because the product is positioned as a foundational daily routine and may feel stimulating or GI-active for some users later in the day.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No formal cycling is needed because this is primarily a daily nutritional coverage product rather than an acute signaling compound.

known_interactions.description: Greens powders often contain vitamin K and other botanicals that may complicate anticoagulant dosing stability.
Minerals and fiber-containing blends can impair thyroid medication absorption if taken too close together.

synergies.mechanism: AG1 offers broad micronutrient and digestive support while omega-3 adds a more targeted anti-inflammatory and cardiometabolic layer.

synergies.benefit: Fills a common foundational gap more explicitly
synergies.mechanism: A dedicated D3 supplement can complement a broad greens formula when a user needs more reliable vitamin D coverage than a blend may provide.

ai_profile.safety_notes: AG1 is generally a convenience product rather than a highly targeted protocol tool. The biggest limitations are proprietary-blend opacity, variable GI tolerance, and the possibility of overlapping ingredients with other supplements or medications.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence for a product like AG1 is mostly indirect, coming from its individual ingredients rather than robust independent trials on the complete formula.

name: Acacia Fiber
description: A gentle soluble prebiotic fiber used to support bowel regularity, satiety, and beneficial gut bacteria with relatively low bloating compared with harsher fibers.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Acacia fiber is a fermentable soluble fiber that helps feed beneficial gut microbes and can modestly improve stool consistency, satiety, and glycemic stability. Compared with some other prebiotic fibers, it is often tolerated with less bloating while still supporting short-chain-fatty-acid production in the colon.

bioavailability.notes: Acacia fiber acts locally in the GI tract rather than through classic systemic absorption. Its main determinant of success is dose tolerance and consistent intake.

half_life.notes: Its benefits come from transit, fermentation, and microbiome effects rather than plasma persistence.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Mix with water or add to yogurt, smoothies, or food. Extra fluids improve tolerance and reduce the risk of constipation or cramping.
timing_recommendations.notes: Any time works, but daily consistency and hydration matter more than exact clock timing.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Prebiotic fiber is generally used continuously rather than cycled, with dose adjustments based on tolerance and bowel response.

known_interactions.description: Like other fibers, acacia may slow or reduce absorption of some medications if taken at the same time. Separating by 1-2 hours is prudent.

synergies.mechanism: Acacia fiber can provide fermentable substrate that helps beneficial microbes establish and function.
Fiber can improve stool form while magnesium citrate supports motility, though the combination should be titrated carefully.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Acacia fiber is usually gentle, but dose should still be increased gradually and paired with adequate water intake. Patients with bowel obstruction or severe GI motility problems need medical guidance before using bulk fibers.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence supports acacia fiber as a reasonably well-tolerated soluble prebiotic for bowel regularity and microbiome support, though it is less extensively studied than some more common fibers.

name: Activated Charcoal
description: A broad-spectrum adsorbent used mainly for short-term GI binding, gas reduction, and occasional detox-style protocols, not for routine foundational supplementation.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Activated charcoal has a highly porous surface area that adsorbs a wide range of compounds inside the GI tract. Its primary utility is local binding in the gut rather than systemic detoxification, which is why it is more useful for acute ingestion scenarios, occasional binder use, and some gas-related complaints than as a daily wellness supplement.

bioavailability.notes: Activated charcoal is not intended to be absorbed. Its effect depends on physical contact with compounds in the GI tract, which is why timing away from food, supplements, and medications is critical.

half_life.notes: Its utility is local to the gut lumen and tied to transit time rather than systemic half-life.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Take it at least 2 hours away from meals, medications, and supplements because it can bind desirable compounds as well as unwanted ones.
timing_recommendations.notes: It is best reserved for occasional, intentional use rather than routine daily stacking.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Daily long-term use is usually not ideal because charcoal can reduce absorption of nutrients and medications. Short targeted use is more appropriate.

known_interactions.description: Activated charcoal can adsorb many medications and sharply reduce their absorption if taken near the same time.
It can also bind beneficial supplements and reduce nutrient uptake when used too close to them.

synergies.mechanism: Hydration helps reduce the risk of constipation and discomfort during binder use.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Activated charcoal is useful for very specific GI-binding situations, but it is a poor choice for casual daily use because it can interfere with medications and nutrition. Separation timing is the main safety rule.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strongest for acute toxicology and GI adsorption use cases. Support for routine wellness detox use is much weaker and more situational.

name: Aged-cheese, red meat, rich choline/histadine/carnitine foods
description: A dietary exposure pattern relevant to TMAO, histamine, and cardiometabolic discussions rather than a beneficial supplement in the usual sense.

common_names: Aged Cheese and Red Meat Pattern
ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This entry represents a cluster of foods that can matter in certain protocols because they may increase histamine load, provide large amounts of choline or carnitine that feed gut-microbial TMAO pathways, or aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals. It is best treated as a dietary exposure flag rather than a universally negative food group.

bioavailability.notes: Nutrients and biogenic amines in these foods are generally bioavailable. The practical question is not absorption but whether the dietary pattern meaningfully changes gut-metabolite output or symptom burden in the individual user.

half_life.notes: This is a food-pattern exposure rather than a single compound with a single half-life.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Pairing these foods with a high-fiber, plant-rich meal may be more favorable than consuming them as the dominant feature of the diet.
timing_recommendations.notes: This entry is more useful for awareness and moderation than for clock-based dosing.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: The better framework is dietary moderation and context rather than cycling.

known_interactions.description: Aged cheeses and certain processed meats can aggravate symptoms in people with histamine intolerance.
Very high intake of red meat and carnitine- or choline-rich foods may be less favorable in people intentionally minimizing TMAO-related dietary exposure.

synergies.mechanism: A plant-rich, high-fiber dietary pattern may moderate some microbiome-mediated downstream effects associated with heavy reliance on animal foods.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is not a classic supplement entry and should be interpreted as a cautionary dietary pattern marker. It may be completely acceptable in moderation for some users and problematic for others depending on cardiometabolic context, histamine sensitivity, and overall diet quality.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is mixed and highly context-dependent, with the strongest rationale coming from dietary-pattern research, TMAO-related microbiome literature, and histamine-sensitivity discussions rather than a single unified intervention trial.

name: Allicin (Garlic Extract)
description: A stabilized garlic-derived antimicrobial and cardiometabolic support compound often used for gut overgrowth, immune support, and vascular health.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Allicin is a reactive sulfur-containing garlic compound with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and vascular-support effects. In supplement form it is often used for GI microbial balance, general immune support, and mild endothelial or lipid-related benefits, though effects vary by extraction method and allicin yield.

bioavailability.notes: Allicin chemistry is highly dependent on processing and stabilization. Enteric or stabilized preparations are often preferred for predictable delivery, while raw garlic equivalence can be difficult to estimate.

half_life.notes: Parent allicin is relatively reactive and short-lived, so product formulation matters for practical effects.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Taking it with food often improves tolerability and reduces reflux or garlic burps.
timing_recommendations.notes: Split dosing can be useful in gut-focused protocols when higher amounts are used.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Shorter targeted runs are common when allicin is being used more like a GI antimicrobial than a simple daily wellness supplement.

known_interactions.description: Garlic compounds may increase bleeding tendency in susceptible patients or when combined with blood-thinning medications.
Higher-dose garlic extracts are usually stopped before surgery because of possible bleeding effects.

synergies.mechanism: These compounds act through different phytochemical profiles and are sometimes paired in short targeted gut protocols.
Berberine can add metabolic and antimicrobial pressure while allicin contributes sulfur-based antimicrobial effects.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Allicin can be useful, but formulation quality matters and GI tolerability varies. Use extra caution with anticoagulants, planned surgery, or aggressive multi-antimicrobial stacks.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence supports antimicrobial and cardiometabolic relevance for garlic-derived compounds, but supplement outcomes vary meaningfully based on extract stability, dose, and preparation.

name: Allicin (Garlic)
description: Whole-garlic or garlic-derived allicin exposure used for cardiovascular support, microbial balance, and general anti-inflammatory coverage.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Allicin is the reactive sulfur compound formed when garlic is crushed or processed. It may support endothelial function, mild lipid and blood-pressure improvement, and broad antimicrobial effects, though real-world potency varies a lot depending on preparation and stabilization.

bioavailability.notes: Garlic sulfur chemistry is highly preparation-dependent. Whole-food garlic, powdered extracts, and aged-garlic extracts do not behave identically, which is why standardization matters.

half_life.notes: Parent sulfur compounds are relatively short-lived, but downstream vascular and inflammatory effects can persist beyond direct plasma exposure.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Taking garlic preparations with meals usually improves comfort and reduces reflux or odor-related burping.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning or afternoon use is practical when the goal is daytime cardiometabolic support.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No established need to cycle garlic for general wellness use, though short targeted antimicrobial phases are also common.

known_interactions.description: Garlic compounds may increase bleeding tendency when combined with blood-thinning medications.
Higher-dose garlic products are often stopped before surgery because of possible bleeding effects.

synergies.mechanism: Garlic and omega-3 work through different pathways that may together support inflammation balance and vascular health.
Vitamin C adds antioxidant coverage while garlic compounds contribute sulfur-based vascular and immune effects.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Garlic is broadly useful but still deserves respect in users on anticoagulants, before surgery, or with sensitive GI tracts. Preparation quality matters more than the label alone.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence supports modest cardiovascular and antimicrobial benefits for garlic preparations, though effect sizes and consistency depend heavily on formulation and dosing.

name: Alpha Linoleic acid (conjugated) - flaxseed oil
description: A plant omega-3 exposure entry that is best interpreted as alpha-linolenic acid from flaxseed oil, used for foundational anti-inflammatory and cardiometabolic support.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Alpha-linolenic acid is a plant-derived omega-3 fatty acid that can contribute to membrane health and inflammatory balance. Some ALA can convert into EPA and DHA, but that conversion is limited, so flaxseed oil is better viewed as a useful plant omega-3 input rather than a full replacement for marine omega-3s.

bioavailability.notes: ALA is well absorbed, but conversion into EPA and DHA is limited. Freshness matters because flax oil oxidizes easily.

half_life.notes: As a fatty acid, tissue effects depend more on consistent dietary intake than a simple plasma half-life.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Take flaxseed oil with meals and avoid high heat cooking if using the oil directly.
timing_recommendations.notes: Meal-based use is the most practical default and helps fit it into a foundational nutrition routine.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No cycling is needed because this is a foundational fatty-acid input rather than an acute signaling supplement.

known_interactions.description: Higher intakes of omega-3-rich oils may modestly increase bleeding tendency in susceptible users.

synergies.mechanism: Marine omega-3s provide direct EPA and DHA while flax supplies plant-derived ALA, creating a more complete omega-3 pattern.
Polyphenols and omega-3 fats can complement each other in supporting oxidative and inflammatory balance.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Flaxseed oil is generally safe, but freshness and expectations matter. It can be a good foundational fat source, yet it should not be oversold as interchangeable with EPA/DHA-rich fish oil.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence supports ALA as a reasonable plant omega-3 input, but the health effect profile is more modest and indirect than direct EPA/DHA supplementation.

name: Alpha-GPC
description: A highly bioavailable choline donor used for focus, memory, and acetylcholine support in cognitively demanding settings.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Alpha-GPC is a choline donor that crosses the blood-brain barrier efficiently and supports acetylcholine synthesis. It is used for attention, memory, neuromuscular signaling, and sometimes pre-workout cognitive sharpness, though dose response and tolerability vary across individuals.

bioavailability.notes: Alpha-GPC is one of the more brain-accessible choline forms. It generally works better as a direct choline donor than broad phospholipid products when the goal is acute acetylcholine support.

half_life.notes: Useful effects are often felt within the same day, particularly when taken for focus or performance support.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food. Food may help if it causes nausea, but many users prefer it earlier in the day for sharper mental effects.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning or midday is usually best because Alpha-GPC can feel mentally activating in some users.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Some users prefer not to run daily high-dose cholinergic support continuously if headaches, muscle tension, or diminishing returns appear.

known_interactions.description: Additional cholinergic load may increase headaches, GI symptoms, or overstimulation in susceptible users.

synergies.mechanism: Alpha-GPC and citicoline both donate choline but through somewhat different phospholipid contexts, though stacking them should be approached carefully.
Caffeine can increase alertness while Alpha-GPC supports acetylcholine-related attention and task engagement.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Alpha-GPC can be useful for cognition, but too much cholinergic drive can backfire. Headache and brain fog often mean the dose is too high or the user does not need additional choline.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is reasonably supportive for cognition and some performance use cases, though it is not as universally robust as foundational sleep, creatine, or omega-3 data.

name: Alpha-Lipoic Acid
description: A mitochondrial cofactor and antioxidant used for energy metabolism, glucose handling, and oxidative stress support.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Alpha-lipoic acid is both a mitochondrial cofactor and a redox-active antioxidant that can regenerate other antioxidants such as vitamins C and E. It is commonly used to support glucose metabolism, mitochondrial function, and oxidative stress handling, with R-ALA often marketed as the more bioactive isomer.

bioavailability.notes: Absorption can be variable and may be improved when taken away from heavy meals. R-ALA formulations are often marketed as more potent, but stability can vary by product.

half_life.notes: Circulating half-life is short, but metabolic effects can extend beyond direct plasma exposure.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Often taken on an empty stomach for best absorption, though a small meal is reasonable if it causes nausea.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning dosing fits best when the goal is daytime glucose or energy support.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No standard cycling protocol exists, though dose and need should be reassessed periodically.

known_interactions.description: Alpha-lipoic acid may modestly improve glucose handling and could require extra monitoring in users already on glucose-lowering therapies.
Some clinicians prefer spacing ALA away from thyroid medication and monitoring symptoms because both can affect energy and metabolic tone.

synergies.mechanism: ALA supports mitochondrial redox chemistry while carnitine supports fatty-acid transport into mitochondria.
These two are often paired in metabolic-health and neuropathy-oriented support protocols.

ai_profile.safety_notes: ALA is generally useful and well tolerated, but glucose-sensitive users should start low and pay attention to dizziness, shakiness, or nausea when dosing fasting.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is fairly solid for antioxidant and metabolic relevance, with some stronger support in neuropathy-oriented contexts than in general longevity claims.

name: Amino-1MQ
description: An emerging-body-composition compound discussed for metabolic support and reduced fat gain, but still backed mainly by preclinical and early-stage evidence.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Amino-1MQ is discussed as an NNMT-inhibiting compound intended to influence cellular energy handling and body-composition signaling. The core rationale is mechanistic and preclinical, with the idea that changing NNMT activity may alter nicotinamide metabolism and metabolic efficiency, but human evidence remains sparse.

bioavailability.notes: Reliable human pharmacokinetic data are limited. Practical use depends heavily on vendor quality and the fact that human dosing guidance is still exploratory.

half_life.notes: A precise human half-life is not well established in the current public literature.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: There is no strong food rule established. Taking it with breakfast is a practical conservative default.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning use is the most sensible default when the goal is energy or metabolic support.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Given the limited human safety and outcome data, cyclical exploratory use with reassessment is more defensible than indefinite continuous use.

synergies.mechanism: Because the evidence base is still emerging, a stable diet-and-training background makes it easier to judge whether Amino-1MQ is doing anything useful.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Amino-1MQ should be treated as experimental relative to mainstream supplements. The key risk is uncertainty: long-term safety, ideal dosing, and real-world efficacy are not yet well defined in humans.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The rationale is interesting but still mostly mechanistic and preclinical, with limited human data compared with better-established metabolic supplements.

name: Anthocyanins (blueberries, açai)
description: Polyphenol-rich pigment compounds from berries and deeply colored fruits used for antioxidant, vascular, and healthy-aging support.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Anthocyanins are flavonoid pigments that can support antioxidant defenses, endothelial function, and cell-signaling pathways related to inflammation and vascular resilience. Their value usually comes from repeated intake through foods or standardized extracts rather than a single large dose.

bioavailability.notes: Anthocyanin bioavailability is variable and many benefits may depend on metabolites and repeated dietary exposure rather than high plasma levels of the parent compounds.

half_life.notes: Parent compounds clear relatively quickly, but repeated intake may support longer-term vascular and signaling effects.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Anthocyanins are commonly consumed with meals or as part of whole foods like berries.
timing_recommendations.notes: Any daytime timing works; consistency and food quality matter more than clock timing.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: These are best treated as repeatable nutritional polyphenols rather than compounds that need cycling.

synergies.mechanism: Polyphenols and omega-3 fats can complement each other in cardiometabolic and endothelial-support patterns.
Different polyphenol classes may support vascular health through partially overlapping but still complementary pathways.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Anthocyanin-rich foods and extracts are generally safe and best framed as a high-quality nutrition layer with plausible healthy-aging relevance rather than a high-risk intervention.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is supportive for vascular, antioxidant, and some cognitive-adjacent benefits, though outcomes depend on food quality, total diet, and the specific extract used.

name: Apigenin
description: A calming flavonoid commonly used for sleep onset, relaxation, and anti-inflammatory support.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Apigenin is a flavonoid found in chamomile, parsley, and celery that appears to support calming effects partly through GABA-related signaling. It also has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which is why it appears both in sleep stacks and broader healthy-aging discussions.

bioavailability.notes: Oral bioavailability is not especially high, but apigenin is still used effectively in bedtime formulas because relatively low doses may be enough for subjective calming effects.

half_life.notes: The practical effect window fits bedtime use better than next-day support.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food, though a light snack is reasonable if it causes stomach sensitivity.
timing_recommendations.notes: Bedtime use is the main pattern when apigenin is being used for calm or sleep onset.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No standard cycling is required, though users can reassess if its sedative value seems to fade.

known_interactions.description: Apigenin may have additive calming or sedative effects alongside other sleep-promoting medications or supplements.

synergies.mechanism: Magnesium and apigenin act through different pathways that can complement bedtime relaxation.
Glycine supports temperature and sleep quality while apigenin adds calming flavonoid signaling.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Apigenin is generally well tolerated and lower-risk, though it is still sensible to be cautious when stacking many sedating agents together.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The mechanistic and traditional rationale is strong, but direct human supplementation literature remains lighter than for better-studied sleep aids such as magnesium or melatonin.

name: Apigenin (Parsley/Celery seeds)
description: A food-derived apigenin entry representing lower-dose botanical sleep and calming support from parsley or celery-seed sources.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This entry reflects apigenin sourced from parsley or celery-seed style botanical preparations. The core calming mechanism is similar to other apigenin products, with potential benefits for sleep onset and bedtime relaxation, but practical potency depends on actual standardization.

bioavailability.notes: Bioavailability is modest and product standardization matters. Food-derived preparations are usually gentler but can also be less predictable than standardized isolates.

half_life.notes: The practical benefit window still fits evening use better than all-day support.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: A light bedtime snack is reasonable if it improves comfort, but food is not required for effect.
timing_recommendations.notes: Evening use remains the clear default.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: This is usually used as a straightforward bedtime support rather than something that needs cycling.

known_interactions.description: Even food-derived apigenin preparations may add to the effect of sedative drugs or strongly calming supplements.

synergies.mechanism: L-theanine promotes relaxed attention while apigenin tilts the stack more directly toward sleep onset.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is generally a low-risk botanical sleep-support option, but standardization and real dose still matter when expectations are high.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The evidence base is modest and mostly inferred from apigenin more broadly rather than a large dedicated literature on parsley- or celery-seed-derived preparations specifically.

name: Apple Cider Vinegar
description: An acetic-acid-rich vinegar used mainly for post-meal glucose support, appetite control, and digestive routine use.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Apple cider vinegar provides acetic acid, which may modestly slow gastric emptying and reduce post-meal glucose excursions. It is used more as a food-adjacent metabolic aid than as a classic supplement, with most interest centered on satiety, postprandial glucose, and digestion rituals.

bioavailability.notes: The relevant active component is acetic acid, which does not require specialized delivery, though liquid forms are usually the most direct. Dilution matters for tolerability and dental safety.

half_life.notes: The practical effect is meal-linked rather than dependent on long circulation.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Usually taken diluted in water shortly before carbohydrate-containing meals.
timing_recommendations.notes: The key timing rule is relative to meals, especially higher-carbohydrate meals.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: ACV is typically used consistently around meals rather than cycled.

known_interactions.description: Combining ACV with glucose-lowering therapies may intensify post-meal glucose reduction in some users.
Large chronic intakes of vinegar are sometimes discussed in relation to potassium balance, so caution is reasonable in susceptible users.

synergies.mechanism: ACV can complement already favorable meal composition by modestly blunting postprandial glucose rise.
Berberine and vinegar target glucose handling through different mechanisms, though the stack should not be overdone.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Apple cider vinegar is low-tech and often useful, but it should be diluted and not oversold. The biggest issues are reflux, throat irritation, and dental erosion from concentrated use.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence supports modest meal-related glucose and satiety benefits, but effects are usually incremental rather than dramatic.

name: Apple vinegar
description: A simpler apple-vinegar entry used for pre-meal glucose support and appetite management through acetic acid exposure.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Apple vinegar works primarily through acetic acid, which may help blunt post-meal glucose excursions and modestly affect satiety. Its practical value is usually highest as a pre-meal routine rather than as a general longevity compound.

bioavailability.notes: This is a straightforward food-derived acid exposure; the biggest determinants of success are timing, dilution, and GI tolerance rather than specialized absorption.

half_life.notes: Meal timing matters more than plasma persistence.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Use before or with meals, diluted in water if liquid.
timing_recommendations.notes: The main rule is to anchor it to meals rather than to a strict time of day.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Routine meal-related use makes more sense than cycling.

known_interactions.description: Adding vinegar to a glucose-lowering regimen may require attention to symptoms of low blood sugar after meals.

synergies.mechanism: Fiber and acetic acid can complement each other in moderating meal-related glucose response.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Useful for some users, but still easy to misuse by taking too much, taking it undiluted, or expecting it to substitute for broader diet quality.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Support is strongest for modest meal-linked metabolic effects, not for large standalone longevity or fat-loss claims.

name: Artemisinin
description: A wormwood-derived compound best known for antiparasitic use, with additional experimental interest in antimicrobial and inflammatory contexts.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Artemisinin is a sesquiterpene lactone from Artemisia annua that is best known for antiparasitic activity. Outside formal malaria treatment contexts it is sometimes discussed for broader antimicrobial or inflammatory applications, but these uses are much less established and should be treated cautiously.

bioavailability.notes: Absorption is formulation-dependent and human supplement use is far less standardized than prescription artemisinin-derived therapies.

half_life.notes: The compound clears relatively quickly, which is one reason some protocols favor split or short-term use.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Taking it with food may improve tolerability, though exact protocol timing is highly context dependent.
timing_recommendations.notes: This is not a casual foundational supplement and should be used, if at all, with a specific rationale.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Artemisinin is generally more defensible as a short targeted phase than as an indefinite daily supplement.

known_interactions.description: Self-supplementation may complicate more formal medical antimicrobial strategies or create confusion about efficacy.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Artemisinin should be treated as a targeted botanical compound, not a general longevity staple. Its strongest role is in medical contexts, and broader supplement use has a much thinner evidence and safety base.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strong in formal antimalarial pharmacology, but much weaker for the broader supplement-style immune or longevity uses often discussed online.

name: Ashwagandha
description: An adaptogenic botanical used for stress resilience, sleep support, and cortisol modulation.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Ashwagandha is an adaptogenic root extract used to support stress resilience through HPA-axis and cortisol-related effects. It may also support sleep quality, subjective calm, and in some contexts androgen or thyroid-related physiology, though those secondary effects are more user-specific.

bioavailability.notes: Standardized extracts tend to be more predictable than loose powders. Food can improve comfort, especially at higher doses.

half_life.notes: Subjective effects often build over repeated use rather than after a single dose.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Taking it with meals usually improves tolerability. Evening use may feel more calming for some users.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning use fits stress resilience; evening use fits calming and sleep support.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Cycling is not mandatory, but many users prefer periodic breaks when using adaptogens continuously.

known_interactions.description: Ashwagandha may influence thyroid-related physiology, so caution is warranted in users on thyroid medication.
It may add to the effect of sedatives or strongly calming supplements.

synergies.mechanism: Magnesium and ashwagandha can complement each other through different calming pathways.
Rhodiola often leans more stimulating while ashwagandha is often more calming, though the combination should be matched to the individual.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Ashwagandha is broadly useful but not completely neutral. Thyroid sensitivity, pregnancy, and autoimmune context deserve caution rather than casual self-stacking.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is fairly supportive for stress and sleep-adjacent outcomes, though quality varies by extract and study design.

name: Ashwagandha KSM-66
description: A standardized full-spectrum ashwagandha extract commonly used for stress reduction, sleep support, and hormonal resilience.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: KSM-66 is a branded, standardized ashwagandha extract designed to provide consistent withanolide delivery from the root. It is used for stress resilience, subjective calm, sleep support, and in some cases exercise recovery or male hormonal support, with a more consistent evidence base than generic non-standardized powders.

bioavailability.notes: KSM-66 is standardized and generally more predictable than generic ashwagandha products. Taking it with food often improves comfort.

half_life.notes: Its effects tend to accumulate over repeated use rather than feeling purely acute.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is usually easiest on the stomach. Evening use may be preferred when the aim is sleep and calm.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning or split morning/evening use are both common.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Standardized adaptogen use can still benefit from periodic breaks if tolerance or subjective effect changes.

known_interactions.description: KSM-66 may influence thyroid-related physiology and should be used carefully in thyroid-sensitive users.
Additive calming or sedative effects are possible.

synergies.mechanism: Magnesium and KSM-66 complement each other in calming, sleep, and recovery-oriented routines.
Creatine targets energetic capacity while KSM-66 leans more toward stress resilience and recovery.

ai_profile.safety_notes: KSM-66 is one of the better-characterized ashwagandha extracts, but the same pregnancy, thyroid, and sedative-stacking cautions still apply.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is stronger than for many generic ashwagandha products because the extract is standardized and appears repeatedly in the literature.

name: Aspirin
description: A low-dose antiplatelet and anti-inflammatory drug-like entry that belongs in cardiovascular risk management, not casual longevity self-experimentation.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Aspirin irreversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes and, at lower doses, is most commonly used for antiplatelet activity. It is clinically important in specific cardiovascular contexts, but the same mechanism also creates real bleeding and GI risks, which is why it should not be treated like a casual wellness supplement.

bioavailability.notes: Aspirin is well absorbed orally. Enteric-coated versions may change onset or GI experience but do not remove bleeding risk.

half_life.notes: Plasma half-life is short, but platelet inhibition lasts much longer because the cyclooxygenase effect is irreversible in platelets.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Taking aspirin with food may help GI comfort but does not eliminate ulcer or bleeding risk.
timing_recommendations.notes: If aspirin is being used, timing consistency matters less than correct indication and clinician guidance.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Aspirin use should follow medical indication, not informal cycling patterns.

known_interactions.description: Combining aspirin with other blood-thinning therapies materially increases bleeding risk unless it is part of a supervised medical plan.
Other NSAIDs can increase GI toxicity and may also interfere with aspirin's antiplatelet timing effects.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Aspirin can be valuable in the right cardiovascular context, but it has a real risk profile and should not be framed as a simple general-longevity supplement. Bleeding history, GI risk, and concurrent drugs matter immediately.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strong for selected medical cardiovascular uses, but the risk-benefit tradeoff is highly context-dependent and not automatically favorable for healthy people seeking longevity benefits.

name: Astaxanthin
description: A red carotenoid antioxidant used for skin photoprotection, exercise recovery, and oxidative-stress support.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Astaxanthin is a lipid-soluble carotenoid that helps protect cell membranes from oxidative stress. It is often used for skin photoprotection, eye support, exercise recovery, and broader anti-inflammatory or healthy-aging routines.

bioavailability.notes: Astaxanthin is fat-soluble, so absorption is better with a meal containing fat. Natural algae-derived preparations are commonly preferred over synthetic sources.

half_life.notes: Tissue effects can build with repeated use, especially in skin-support settings.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Take with a fat-containing meal for best absorption.
timing_recommendations.notes: Exact time of day matters less than taking it with food consistently.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Astaxanthin is usually run as a steady daily antioxidant and skin-support compound rather than cycled.

synergies.mechanism: Omega-3s and astaxanthin are often paired in marine-derived products because they complement each other around lipid membranes and inflammation balance.
Collagen supports structural skin proteins while astaxanthin adds antioxidant and photoprotective support.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Astaxanthin is generally low risk and well tolerated. Its main limitation is that benefits are usually incremental and require consistent use rather than producing dramatic acute effects.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is reasonably supportive for oxidative-stress, skin, and recovery contexts, though not as definitive as foundational cardiovascular or sleep compounds.

name: Astaxanthin (seafood)
description: A food-derived astaxanthin exposure entry reflecting carotenoid intake from salmon, shrimp, krill, or other marine foods.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This entry represents dietary astaxanthin coming from seafood rather than a dedicated extract. It contributes antioxidant carotenoid exposure and usually arrives together with protein, omega-3 fats, or other marine nutrients depending on the food source.

bioavailability.notes: Food-derived astaxanthin is naturally paired with fats and phospholipids that may support absorption, though exact intake is harder to quantify than with supplements.

half_life.notes: As with supplemental astaxanthin, repeated intake matters more than single exposure.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Most meaningful when consumed as part of marine foods or alongside fats.
timing_recommendations.notes: This is better viewed as a food-pattern input than a strict dosing supplement.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Whole-food astaxanthin intake is best treated as a dietary pattern rather than something to cycle.

synergies.mechanism: Seafood often co-delivers carotenoids and omega-3 fats in a way that makes the overall pattern more valuable than either alone.

ai_profile.safety_notes: The main practical consideration is food allergy or source quality rather than astaxanthin itself.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence for astaxanthin is supportive overall, though dose precision is much lower when obtained through seafood rather than standardized extract products.

name: Astragalus
description: An adaptogenic and immunomodulatory root used for resilience, healthy aging, and immune support.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Astragalus is a traditional root botanical used for immune resilience, fatigue, and broader healthy-aging support. Interest in longevity circles partly comes from astragalus-derived fractions associated with telomerase-related discussions, though whole-root astragalus should not be equated with highly specialized purified derivatives.

bioavailability.notes: Whole-root astragalus acts as a mixed botanical extract rather than a single purified active. Product quality and extraction style matter.

half_life.notes: As a botanical mixture, there is no single useful half-life.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Usually taken with meals or as part of traditional decoctions.
timing_recommendations.notes: Daytime use is common when astragalus is used for resilience and vitality.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No standard cycling rule is required, though some users rotate adaptogenic botanicals seasonally.

known_interactions.description: Because astragalus may influence immune activity, caution is warranted in users on immune-suppressing therapies.

synergies.mechanism: Astragalus and medicinal mushrooms are often paired in traditional or functional immune-support formulations.
Ashwagandha often tilts more toward stress/cortisol support while astragalus is often positioned more toward vitality and immune resilience.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Astragalus is generally lower risk, but longevity claims are easy to overstate. It is better framed as a resilience and immune-support botanical than as a proven telomere intervention.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is supportive for traditional and immune-resilience use cases, but the strongest longevity claims are still more speculative than settled.

name: Auricularia polytricha
description: A wood-ear mushroom used as a culinary and medicinal mushroom entry for vascular, fiber, and immune support.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Auricularia polytricha is an edible mushroom valued for soluble fiber, polysaccharides, and mushroom-derived bioactives. It is typically discussed for cardiometabolic, gut, and immune-support relevance rather than as a high-potency single-target supplement.

bioavailability.notes: As with many mushrooms, efficacy depends on whether the product is a true fruiting-body extract, a mycelium product, or a whole-food preparation.

half_life.notes: Its value is tied more to nutritional and polysaccharide exposure than to a single circulating molecule.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Most naturally consumed with food or as part of meals.
timing_recommendations.notes: This is better treated as a food-like mushroom input than a sharp timing-dependent supplement.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Mushroom support is usually run as a steady nutrition-adjacent pattern rather than cycled.

known_interactions.description: Some mushroom products are discussed in relation to platelet or bleeding risk, so additional caution is reasonable in heavily anticoagulated users.

synergies.mechanism: Auricularia works best as part of a broader fiber- and plant-rich dietary pattern.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Auricularia is generally a low-risk mushroom entry, but it should be framed as a supportive food-like compound rather than a miracle cardiovascular tool.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The rationale is plausible and supported by preclinical and traditional-use literature, but high-quality modern human supplementation data are still limited.

name: BPL1-HT
description: A heat-treated probiotic or postbiotic strain used for gut and body-composition support with a still-emerging evidence base.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: BPL1-HT is generally positioned as a heat-treated or postbiotic version of the BPL1 strain, intended to influence gut-mediated metabolic signaling rather than acting as a classic live probiotic. Interest centers on body-composition and metabolic-support effects, but the evidence base is still relatively early.

bioavailability.notes: Because this is a postbiotic-style product, viability is not the key issue in the same way it is for live probiotics. What matters more is the specific strain identity and preparation quality.

half_life.notes: Effects are thought to arise from repeated gut signaling rather than a plasma half-life.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can usually be taken with or without food depending on the product, but with-food use is a practical default.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing matters less than consistent daily use.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Microbiome-oriented products are usually assessed over weeks of steady use rather than cycled.

synergies.mechanism: A prebiotic like acacia fiber may complement microbiome-targeted postbiotic or probiotic formulations.

ai_profile.safety_notes: BPL1-HT appears relatively low risk, but it is still a more niche gut-metabolic product and should not be overinterpreted beyond the available strain-specific data.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The evidence is promising but limited, and much of the relevance is tied to specific strain or formulation studies rather than broad microbiome consensus.

name: Bacopa Monnieri
description: An Ayurvedic cognitive botanical used primarily for memory consolidation, stress reduction, and long-term nootropic support.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Bacopa is an Ayurvedic herb best known for supporting memory consolidation and calmer cognitive performance over time. It is not usually an acute stimulant; rather, it appears to work through repeated use involving neuroprotective, antioxidant, and stress-modulating pathways.

bioavailability.notes: Standardized bacoside extracts are preferred for predictability. Taking bacopa with food often improves tolerance.

half_life.notes: Subjective benefits often emerge over weeks rather than after one dose.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Food often improves tolerability and may be especially helpful if bacopa causes nausea.
timing_recommendations.notes: Some users prefer evening use if bacopa feels calming; others place it in the morning as a daily cognitive tonic.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Bacopa is usually assessed over sustained use rather than cycled, because its value comes from cumulative effect.

known_interactions.description: Bacopa can feel calming in some users and may add to sedative burden at the margins.

synergies.mechanism: Alpha-GPC can cover acute cholinergic support while bacopa supports longer-term memory and neurocognitive resilience.
Rhodiola often feels more activating while bacopa often feels steadier and more memory-oriented.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Bacopa is useful but often misunderstood. Its benefits are usually delayed, and GI tolerability is the main reason some users stop early.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is fairly supportive for memory and cognitive-stress use cases, especially with standardized extracts over multi-week use.

name: Beetroot Juice
description: A nitrate-rich performance and vascular support drink used to raise nitric oxide availability, improve blood flow, and support exercise output.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Beetroot juice is valued mainly for its high nitrate content, which can be converted through the nitrate-nitrite-nitric oxide pathway into greater nitric oxide signaling. That can support vasodilation, exercise efficiency, blood flow, and subjective endurance capacity, especially when used around training or consistently in cardiovascular-support routines.

bioavailability.notes: The key determinant is nitrate content rather than the beet juice label alone. Oral bacteria are important for nitrate reduction, so antibacterial mouthwash can blunt the effect.

half_life.notes: Nitrite and nitric oxide signaling rise over hours, while training-related benefits often depend on dosing window and repeated use.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken alone before training or with meals for cardiovascular support. Avoid relying on it right after antiseptic mouthwash use.
timing_recommendations.notes: For exercise use, pre-workout timing matters. For blood-flow support, consistent daily use can also make sense.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No formal cycling is required, though some users reserve concentrated products for training blocks or endurance events.

known_interactions.description: Because beetroot juice can augment nitric oxide signaling and lower blood pressure modestly, caution is sensible when combined with prescription vasodilators.

synergies.mechanism: Beetroot targets the nitrate-nitrite pathway while citrulline supports arginine availability for nitric oxide synthesis.
Electrolytes help support performance and circulation during longer training sessions while beetroot targets blood flow efficiency.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Beetroot juice is generally low risk and practical, but users with recurrent calcium-oxalate stone issues or low blood pressure should be more deliberate with dose and frequency.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strongest for exercise efficiency, blood-flow support, and modest blood-pressure effects, especially when nitrate content is meaningful and timing is handled well.

name: Berberine
description: A plant alkaloid used for blood-glucose, lipid, and metabolic support, often positioned as a supplement analogue to some metformin-like pathways.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Berberine appears to influence AMPK signaling, hepatic glucose output, gut-mediated metabolic signaling, and lipid metabolism. In practice it is used most often for glucose regulation, insulin resistance support, and triglyceride or LDL improvement, with secondary interest in longevity because of its overlap with energy-sensing pathways.

bioavailability.notes: Berberine has relatively poor classic oral bioavailability, but that does not make it ineffective because some of its relevance appears to involve gut and first-pass metabolic effects. Divided dosing is common.

half_life.notes: Its practical use usually favors split dosing with meals rather than one large dose.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Taking it with meals is standard because it improves tolerability and matches its metabolic use case.
timing_recommendations.notes: Meal-linked dosing is usually the most practical default for berberine.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: There is no universal need to cycle it, but periodic reassessment makes sense because prolonged use can be limited by GI issues, medication overlap, or user goals changing.

known_interactions.description: Berberine can add to the glucose-lowering effect of prescription diabetes medications and may require closer monitoring.
Berberine may influence transporter and enzyme activity, so caution is warranted with narrow-therapeutic-index medications.

synergies.mechanism: Fiber can improve meal-response dynamics while berberine targets AMPK-related and gut-metabolic pathways.
Berberine can support glucose and lipid handling while omega-3 adds anti-inflammatory and triglyceride-oriented support.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Berberine is one of the more useful metabolic supplements, but it is also one of the easier ones to use too casually. Medication overlap, GI tolerance, and overly aggressive stacking are the main practical risks.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is reasonably supportive for glucose and lipid biomarkers, though product quality, dosing pattern, and medication context matter more than simple hype suggests.

name: Bergamot
description: A citrus polyphenol extract used mainly for LDL, triglyceride, and cardiovascular risk-factor support.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Bergamot extract is used primarily for cardiometabolic support, especially LDL and triglyceride management. Its polyphenols appear to influence lipid metabolism, oxidative stress, and vascular signaling, making it more of a cholesterol-support botanical than a general citrus wellness ingredient.

bioavailability.notes: Quality depends heavily on a standardized bergamot polyphenol fraction rather than generic citrus powder. It is usually well tolerated with meals.

half_life.notes: As a polyphenol-rich extract, there is no single practical half-life to anchor dosing decisions.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Usually taken with meals for tolerability and routine consistency.
timing_recommendations.notes: Exact timing matters less than daily consistency over weeks.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Bergamot is usually used as a continuous lipid-support tool rather than something to cycle.

known_interactions.description: Bergamot may overlap with lipid-lowering goals, so users on prescription therapy should watch for additive effect or duplicated strategy.

synergies.mechanism: Omega-3 is often stronger for triglycerides while bergamot is often used for LDL and vascular biomarkers.
Fiber affects bile acid and cholesterol handling while bergamot adds a polyphenol and oxidative-stress layer.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Bergamot is usually well tolerated, but it should be framed as a useful lipid-support adjunct rather than a substitute for addressing broader cardiovascular risk or clinical lipid management when those are needed.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is encouraging for lipid biomarkers and cardiovascular-support use, but the literature is smaller and less settled than it is for foundational diet, fiber, or prescription lipid approaches.

name: Beta alanine
description: A carnosine-building performance amino acid used to buffer muscular acidity and improve repeated high-intensity output.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Beta-alanine raises intramuscular carnosine over time. Higher carnosine helps buffer acid accumulation during repeated high-intensity effort, which can improve muscular endurance and training output in intervals, sprint work, and resistance sets that generate substantial metabolic stress.

bioavailability.notes: The main issue is not poor absorption but dose-related tingling. Splitting the daily dose or using sustained-release products improves practicality.

half_life.notes: The acute half-life matters less than the cumulative rise in muscle carnosine over weeks.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food, though food may reduce paresthesia in some users.
timing_recommendations.notes: Unlike caffeine, beta-alanine is not primarily an acute stimulant. Consistent daily loading matters more than exact pre-workout timing.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: There is no need to cycle it, though some users reserve it for training blocks where high-intensity buffering is especially valuable.

synergies.mechanism: Creatine supports phosphocreatine regeneration while beta-alanine supports acid buffering during harder interval or repeated efforts.
Beetroot can help circulatory efficiency while beta-alanine targets muscular buffering capacity.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Beta-alanine is usually low risk. The main reason users stop is the harmless but sometimes unpleasant tingling sensation, which is dose-related and manageable.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is fairly solid for repeated high-intensity performance support, though benefits are more specific to training style than general energy hype suggests.

name: Beta-Alanine
description: A sports-performance amino acid that builds muscle carnosine and helps delay fatigue in repeated high-intensity work.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Beta-alanine increases muscle carnosine stores over time, helping buffer acidity generated during repeated hard efforts. It is best suited to sprint intervals, high-rep resistance work, and sports where repeated bursts create muscular burn and fatigue.

bioavailability.notes: Daily muscle loading is what matters. The main practical limiter is paresthesia from large single servings.

half_life.notes: Muscle carnosine accumulation is the real target, so acute plasma kinetics are secondary.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Food is optional, but it can make larger doses feel smoother.
timing_recommendations.notes: This is best thought of as a daily loading supplement, not a one-shot ergogenic only for the session itself.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: It can be used continuously or during focused training blocks without a required cycle.

synergies.mechanism: Creatine helps short explosive output while beta-alanine helps maintain output as acid load builds.

ai_profile.safety_notes: The main tradeoff is comfort, not danger. Split dosing fixes most issues for users who otherwise tolerate it well.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The literature supports beta-alanine for specific exercise contexts, especially repeated high-intensity work, rather than for generic all-day energy.

name: Betaine (TMG)
description: A trimethylglycine methyl donor used for homocysteine balance, liver support, and in some settings strength or power performance.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: TMG donates methyl groups in one-carbon metabolism and can help support homocysteine recycling while also acting as an osmolyte. In practice it is used for methylation support, liver-related support, and sometimes power or body-composition protocols, though the performance literature is more mixed than the methyl-donor rationale.

bioavailability.notes: TMG is orally practical and usually easy to absorb. The main variables are dose, GI tolerance, and whether the user actually needs methyl-donor support.

half_life.notes: Exact kinetics vary, but once- or twice-daily dosing is usually practical.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food, though meals often improve comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning use is common for methylation and performance-oriented routines.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No universal cycling is needed, but reassessment is useful if the user is already on multiple methyl-donor compounds or has no clear reason to keep it.

known_interactions.description: TMG is generally safe, but stacking it aggressively with other methyl donors may be unnecessary or make some users feel overstimulated or off.

synergies.mechanism: TMG complements other one-carbon nutrients involved in methylation and homocysteine handling.

synergies.benefit: Possible methylation sparing plus performance support
synergies.mechanism: Creatine synthesis draws on methyl groups, so creatine and TMG are sometimes paired in performance or methylation-aware protocols.

ai_profile.safety_notes: TMG is useful when there is a real methylation, homocysteine, or performance rationale, but it does not need to be part of every stack. Clear purpose matters more than novelty here.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strongest for methylation and homocysteine-related use, with more mixed but still plausible support for some exercise and body-composition applications.

name: Betaine HCl
description: A stomach-acid support supplement used to improve protein digestion and help users with suspected low gastric acidity tolerate heavier meals.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Betaine HCl is used to temporarily increase stomach acidity at mealtime. The rationale is that some users with low gastric acid output may digest protein more effectively and feel less post-meal heaviness when gastric acidity is improved, though this is a symptom-guided use case rather than a universally indicated supplement.

bioavailability.notes: This is a local gastric support tool rather than a systemic nutrient strategy. Meal context and stomach lining tolerance matter far more than classic absorption issues.

half_life.notes: Its functional effect is meal-bound rather than meaningfully described by systemic half-life.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Use only with meals, especially meals containing meaningful protein. Do not take it by itself.
timing_recommendations.notes: If used, it should be tied directly to meals rather than treated like a general daily supplement.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: It is usually used situationally or reassessed over time rather than formally cycled.

known_interactions.description: Using betaine HCl while deliberately suppressing stomach acid can create a contradictory strategy and may worsen irritation.
Extra caution is warranted if the stomach lining is already vulnerable or irritated.

synergies.mechanism: Stomach acid support and digestive enzymes target different parts of the digestive process and are often paired in meal support protocols.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Betaine HCl can be genuinely useful for a narrow use case, but it is easy to misuse. If it causes burning, pain, or reflux, it is the wrong tool in that moment.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is much thinner than user anecdotes suggest. This is better treated as a practical digestive experiment than a strongly validated therapy.

name: Betaine TMG
description: A trimethylglycine methyl donor used for homocysteine balance, methylation support, and liver or performance-oriented protocols.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: TMG acts as a methyl donor inside one-carbon metabolism, helping recycle homocysteine and support methylation-dependent physiology. It is also discussed for liver support and sometimes exercise performance, though the clearest rationale is usually methylation and homocysteine management rather than digestion itself.

bioavailability.notes: TMG is generally well absorbed and easy to use orally. The main question is whether there is an actual need for a methyl donor, not whether it gets in.

half_life.notes: Once- or twice-daily dosing is generally practical.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals are optional but often improve comfort and adherence.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning placement makes sense for users who find methyl donors slightly activating.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: TMG is usually run continuously when indicated, with periodic reassessment based on labs or goals.

known_interactions.description: Aggressively stacking multiple methyl donors may be unnecessary and can feel overstimulating or simply redundant.

synergies.mechanism: These nutrients work in adjacent methylation pathways and are often paired when homocysteine balance is the goal.

ai_profile.safety_notes: TMG is useful when it serves a real purpose, but there is no reason to force it into every protocol. It is best used deliberately, not because it sounds advanced.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The evidence is most credible around homocysteine and methylation support, with broader longevity claims remaining more inferential.

name: Bilberry
description: An anthocyanin-rich berry extract used mainly for eye comfort, capillary support, and antioxidant coverage.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Bilberry provides anthocyanins and polyphenols associated with antioxidant activity and microvascular support. It is most often positioned for eye fatigue, retinal support, and capillary resilience rather than as a broad anti-aging compound with large-scale outcome data.

bioavailability.notes: Standardized anthocyanin content matters more than generic berry branding. Whole-food bilberries are valuable nutritionally, but extract products are usually chosen for concentrated anthocyanin delivery.

half_life.notes: Polyphenol kinetics vary, and practical use is based more on repeated intake than single-dose pharmacokinetics.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Taking it with meals is a simple default for consistency and comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is flexible; steady use matters more than clock precision.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Bilberry is usually used as a steady antioxidant or eye-support supplement rather than cycled.

known_interactions.description: Polyphenol-rich extracts are usually low risk, but extra caution is reasonable in heavily anticoagulated users.

synergies.mechanism: Bilberry contributes anthocyanin and microvascular support while lutein and zeaxanthin target macular carotenoid status.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Bilberry is generally low risk. The main limitation is inflated marketing relative to the modest but plausible eye-support evidence.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is supportive for eye-fatigue and vascular-support narratives, but much of the enthusiasm exceeds the size of the modern human trial base.

name: Biotin
description: Vitamin B7 used mainly for deficiency correction and sometimes for hair or nail support, though benefits in replete users are often overstated.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Biotin is a water-soluble B vitamin involved in carboxylase-dependent metabolism and normal hair, skin, and nail physiology. In practice, the clearest benefits appear when biotin deficiency is present or likely, while visible improvements in already replete users are less predictable.

bioavailability.notes: Biotin is generally absorbed well orally. The bigger issue is whether more biotin is actually useful, not whether it enters the system.

half_life.notes: Because it is water-soluble, practical use is usually simple once-daily dosing.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is not a major lever for biotin.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No cycling is required, but supplementation should be reassessed if there is no deficiency or no visible reason to continue.

known_interactions.description: High-dose biotin can distort important blood test results and should be disclosed and often held before lab work.

synergies.benefit: Better hair and nail substrate support
synergies.mechanism: Biotin does not replace the need for adequate protein, iron, zinc, and overall nutritional sufficiency when hair or nail concerns are the goal.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Biotin is usually safe, but high-dose use is often more marketing-driven than clinically necessary. Lab interference is the main serious practical concern.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strongest for deficiency states. Outside that context, benefits are inconsistent and far less impressive than beauty-supplement marketing implies.

name: Black Garlic
description: An aged garlic preparation used for antioxidant, vascular, and cardiometabolic support with less odor and stomach harshness than raw garlic.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Black garlic is produced by aging garlic under controlled heat and humidity, which changes its flavor profile and alters its sulfur compounds and antioxidant matrix. It is generally positioned for vascular, antioxidant, and general healthy-aging support with better tolerability than raw garlic.

bioavailability.notes: Quality depends on preparation method and whether the product is a food-style black garlic or a standardized extract.

half_life.notes: As a complex aged food-botanical matrix, there is no single useful half-life.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Usually easiest to tolerate with meals.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is flexible; repeated intake matters more than clock timing.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Black garlic is usually used as a steady food-botanical support rather than cycled.

known_interactions.description: Garlic-derived products can modestly increase bleeding concern in some contexts, so extra caution is sensible around surgery or anticoagulation.

synergies.mechanism: Bergamot is often used for lipid support while black garlic contributes antioxidant and vascular-support signals.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Black garlic is usually gentler than raw garlic, but it should still be treated as a cardiovascular-support adjunct, not a substitute for broader diet and clinical care when risk is meaningful.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is promising for antioxidant and cardiovascular-support biomarkers, but the human literature remains smaller than marketing language often suggests.

name: Black Seed Oil
description: Nigella sativa seed oil used for broad anti-inflammatory, immune, and cardiometabolic support, with thymoquinone as its best-known active constituent.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Black seed oil provides thymoquinone and other bioactive compounds associated with anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and immune-modulating effects. It is used broadly across immune, metabolic, and general wellness goals, though the breadth of its reputation is larger than the precision of the evidence.

bioavailability.notes: As an oil-based product, quality, oxidation control, and thymoquinone content matter. Taking it with meals often improves comfort.

half_life.notes: A mixed oil extract does not have a single clinically useful half-life.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals generally improve tolerance and fit its oil-based delivery.
timing_recommendations.notes: Exact timing is secondary to quality and consistent intake.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No established cycling pattern is required, though some users prefer running it in focused immune or inflammation blocks.

known_interactions.description: Black seed oil may overlap modestly with blood sugar and blood pressure support, so additional monitoring is reasonable if medications are already in place.

synergies.mechanism: Omega-3 and black seed oil contribute different anti-inflammatory and membrane-related inputs.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Black seed oil is versatile and usually well tolerated, but it is often oversold as a cure-all. Quality and realistic expectations matter.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is broad but uneven, with promising results across inflammation and metabolic markers yet fewer large rigorous trials than its popularity might imply.

name: Boron
description: A trace mineral used for bone, joint, and hormone-support goals, often in low-dose daily protocols rather than high-dose interventions.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Boron is a trace mineral involved in mineral metabolism, bone physiology, inflammatory tone, and possibly steroid-hormone dynamics. In practice it is usually used for bone and joint support, with secondary interest in free testosterone, magnesium handling, and vitamin D-related physiology.

bioavailability.notes: Boron is generally easy to absorb orally at low doses. The main consideration is not absorption but avoiding unnecessary high-dose use.

half_life.notes: Routine once-daily dosing is generally practical.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken with food for routine convenience.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is not a major lever for boron.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Low-dose mineral support usually does not require cycling, though very high doses are unnecessary for most users.

known_interactions.description: Because boron is sometimes discussed in relation to steroid-hormone dynamics, extra caution is sensible in users managing hormone-sensitive conditions.

synergies.mechanism: Boron and magnesium are often paired in bone and muscle-oriented mineral protocols.
Boron is often used as a trace-mineral complement to larger bone-health nutrients.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Boron is generally low risk at sensible doses. The main mistake is turning a useful trace mineral into an unnecessarily aggressive hormone experiment.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is supportive for bone and mineral metabolism with some interesting hormone-related signals, but the stronger endocrine claims remain more suggestive than settled.

name: Boswellia Serrata
description: A frankincense-derived extract used primarily for joint comfort and inflammatory tone, with additional relevance for gut inflammatory support.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Boswellia serrata contains boswellic acids, including AKBA, that appear to influence inflammatory signaling, especially 5-lipoxygenase-related pathways. It is most often used for joint comfort, mobility, and inflammatory bowel-support contexts rather than as a generic antioxidant.

bioavailability.notes: Standardized extracts with clear boswellic-acid content are more predictable than raw powders. Some enhanced formulations are designed specifically to improve absorption.

half_life.notes: Practical use is usually based on repeated anti-inflammatory support rather than exact half-life management.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals often improve tolerability.
timing_recommendations.notes: Once- or twice-daily with-food use is typical.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Boswellia is generally used steadily while monitoring symptom response rather than cycled.

known_interactions.description: Extra caution is reasonable when combining multiple botanical anti-inflammatory agents with bleeding-risk medications.
