synergies.mechanism: Curcumin and boswellia are often paired because they influence overlapping but nonidentical inflammatory pathways.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Boswellia is often a strong non-pharmaceutical option for joint comfort, but extract quality matters and it is still a symptom-support tool rather than a cure for structural joint problems.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is reasonably supportive for joint discomfort and mobility, with additional promise in gut-inflammatory contexts when standardized extracts are used.

name: Broccomax / Sulforaphane
description: A glucoraphanin and sulforaphane-support supplement used for Nrf2 activation, cellular stress resilience, and healthy-aging-oriented detoxification signaling.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Sulforaphane is an isothiocyanate derived from glucoraphanin when myrosinase activity is present. It is studied for activating Nrf2-related cytoprotective signaling, modulating oxidative stress response, and supporting endogenous detoxification and cellular stress resilience rather than supplying a direct antioxidant payload.

bioavailability.notes: Whether the product provides active sulforaphane or only glucoraphanin matters enormously. Myrosinase availability is a key determinant of conversion efficiency.

half_life.notes: The signaling effects may outlast direct plasma exposure because the main value lies in downstream pathway activation.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken with food. Some users pair broccoli-sprout products with mustard-seed-derived myrosinase sources when using whole-food formats.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning placement is common in healthy-aging routines, but consistency and conversion quality matter more than exact timing.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Some users cycle sulforaphane-style compounds because they are valued as hormetic signaling agents rather than nutrients that must be present continuously.

known_interactions.description: Routine doses are usually fine, but users with complex thyroid management sometimes prefer a more deliberate approach to concentrated cruciferous compounds.

synergies.mechanism: Green tea polyphenols and sulforaphane both support stress-response biology through different signaling pathways.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Sulforaphane is one of the more interesting healthy-aging compounds, but product quality and conversion biology matter so much that label claims can be misleading. It is a precision-quality problem more than a hype problem.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Mechanistic and early human data are compelling, especially around Nrf2-related signaling, but large long-term outcome evidence is still limited.

name: Butyrate
description: A short-chain fatty acid supplement used to support gut-barrier integrity, colonocyte health, and inflammatory balance.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Butyrate is a short-chain fatty acid naturally produced when beneficial gut microbes ferment fiber. Supplemental butyrate or tributyrin is used to support colonocyte energy, intestinal barrier function, and inflammatory balance, particularly when diet or microbiome function may not be delivering enough endogenous production.

bioavailability.notes: Delivery format matters because butyrate is best valued for lower-GI relevance. Some forms emphasize delayed release or tributyrin to improve practicality and tolerability.

half_life.notes: Its main relevance is local gut exposure rather than a traditional systemic half-life.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals often improve comfort. It works best as a complement to a fiber-supportive diet, not a substitute for it.
timing_recommendations.notes: Steady routine use matters more than exact timing.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Butyrate is generally used as a steady gut-support tool rather than cycled.

synergies.mechanism: Fiber helps feed butyrate-producing microbes while supplemental butyrate offers a more direct short-chain-fatty-acid input.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Butyrate is conceptually strong, but the best long-term strategy is still building endogenous production through diet and microbiome support. Supplements are an adjunct, not the whole answer.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Mechanistic rationale is strong and clinically plausible, especially for barrier and inflammatory support, though high-quality human supplementation data are still growing.

name: C60 Fullerene
description: A carbon-60 compound sometimes marketed for antioxidant and longevity support, but with highly speculative human relevance and limited safety clarity.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: C60 fullerene is a nanocarbon molecule discussed as a redox-active or free-radical-handling compound, often in oil-based preparations. Most of the enthusiasm comes from preclinical work and mechanistic speculation rather than meaningful human longevity evidence, so it should be treated as experimental rather than established.

bioavailability.notes: Bioavailability depends heavily on the solvent or carrier oil and product quality. The bigger issue is not optimizing exposure but the lack of robust human pharmacology and safety data.

half_life.notes: Human pharmacokinetic data are too limited for confident half-life guidance.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Most commercial preparations are oil-based and taken with food.
timing_recommendations.notes: There is no evidence-based timing strategy; routine claims are mostly anecdotal.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Because long-term safety and utility remain unclear, a cautious, non-foundational approach is more defensible than continuous use.

ai_profile.safety_notes: C60 fullerene is not a foundational supplement. The main issue is not finding the perfect dose but acknowledging that meaningful human efficacy and long-term safety remain uncertain.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Most of the buzz comes from animal and mechanistic literature. Human evidence is sparse, making longevity claims far ahead of the proof.

name: C60 Fullerene (Carbon 60 in olive oil)
description: An olive-oil carrier form of carbon 60 marketed for longevity and antioxidant support, with a highly experimental evidence base and unclear long-term human safety.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This preparation disperses C60 fullerene in olive oil, reflecting the format most often associated with the original animal-longevity discussions. The theoretical rationale centers on unusual redox behavior and antioxidant claims, but real human relevance remains uncertain and highly speculative.

bioavailability.notes: Carrier oil, purity, light stability, and manufacturing quality matter more than fine-tuning dose. Human absorption and tissue behavior are still poorly characterized.

half_life.notes: There is not enough human pharmacokinetic clarity to treat this like a mature supplement category.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Because it is already oil-based, it is usually taken with food for comfort and routine consistency.
timing_recommendations.notes: Any timing guidance here is practical rather than evidence-based.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Continuous long-term use is hard to justify given the absence of robust human safety and outcome data.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is best categorized as experimental self-experimentation rather than mainstream longevity practice. Even if the mechanism is interesting, the evidence gap is too large to treat it as routine.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The profile is driven mostly by animal work and internet longevity culture rather than robust human data. Claims should be interpreted very conservatively.

name: CBD
description: A non-intoxicating cannabinoid used mainly for calm, sleep support, and inflammatory symptom management, with meaningful medication-interaction considerations.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: CBD interacts with the endocannabinoid system and a broader set of receptor and signaling pathways associated with stress regulation, inflammation, pain perception, and sleep quality. It is most often used for calm and symptom support rather than for a clear longevity rationale.

bioavailability.notes: Oral CBD bioavailability is variable. Taking it with food, especially fat-containing food, often improves practical exposure.

half_life.notes: Pharmacokinetics vary by dose and formulation, but once- or twice-daily use is common.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Taking CBD with a meal often improves absorption and tolerability.
timing_recommendations.notes: Evening placement makes sense when the main goal is calm or sleep.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No formal cycling is required, though some users reserve CBD for symptomatic periods rather than continuous daily use.

known_interactions.description: CBD can add to drowsiness or calm-inducing medications.
CBD can affect hepatic enzyme activity and medication levels, so drug interaction review matters.

synergies.mechanism: Magnesium supports relaxation through different pathways while CBD targets endocannabinoid-related calming.

ai_profile.safety_notes: CBD can be useful, but product quality and medication interactions are the real issues. It should be treated more like a pharmacologically active compound than a harmless wellness oil.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is meaningful for select clinical contexts and symptom support, but real-world supplement use is limited more by product inconsistency and interactions than by complete lack of signal.

name: Calcium - Alpha Ketoglutarate
description: A calcium-bound alpha-ketoglutarate compound used in healthy-aging circles for mitochondrial and longevity-oriented support, with most enthusiasm still ahead of the human evidence.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Calcium alpha-ketoglutarate combines calcium with a Krebs-cycle intermediate involved in cellular energy metabolism and broader signaling biology. It is discussed in longevity circles because alpha-ketoglutarate has been linked to metabolic, inflammatory, and epigenetic pathways in preclinical work, but the strongest human anti-aging claims remain preliminary.

bioavailability.notes: Oral practicality is reasonable, but product quality and the combined calcium load should be considered. This is more a signaling and healthy-aging compound than a classic acute energy booster.

half_life.notes: The real interest is downstream metabolic signaling, not acute plasma persistence.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Taking it with meals is practical and may improve GI comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning use is common in healthy-aging routines, but timing is not the main issue.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: There is no standard cycling approach, though periodic reassessment is sensible because the human evidence base is still early.

known_interactions.description: Total calcium exposure should be considered, especially in users with stone risk or calcium-sensitive conditions.

synergies.mechanism: Magnesium can help offset the common mistake of focusing only on calcium intake while ignoring broader mineral balance.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Calcium AKG is interesting, but it is still an emerging longevity compound rather than a proven anti-aging anchor. Users should be careful not to ignore the calcium component.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The appeal is driven mainly by mechanistic and early human or preclinical aging data, which is promising but not yet definitive.

name: Calcium AEP
description: A niche calcium aminoethyl phosphate compound sometimes marketed for membrane and neuro-support, but with a thin evidence base and limited modern clinical relevance.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Calcium AEP is an older, relatively obscure compound discussed in orthomolecular and alternative-medicine contexts for membrane support and nervous-system resilience. Its proposed benefits are far more speculative and less validated than those of mainstream nutrients or nootropics.

bioavailability.notes: There is limited modern pharmacokinetic work on calcium AEP, so dosing is based more on legacy use patterns than strong contemporary evidence.

half_life.notes: Human pharmacology is not well characterized.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals are a reasonable default for comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Any timing guidance here is largely pragmatic rather than evidence-driven.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Because the evidence base is thin, it is better treated as a cautious trial than a permanent staple.

known_interactions.description: As a calcium-containing compound, total calcium load still matters.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Calcium AEP is a niche, low-confidence compound. It is not in the same evidence class as mainstream brain or mineral supplements and should be framed accordingly.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Modern clinical evidence is sparse. Most discussion comes from older alternative-health traditions rather than robust contemporary human trials.

name: Calcium Alpha-Ketoglutarate
description: A calcium-bound AKG compound used in longevity-focused protocols for mitochondrial and healthy-aging support, with a more interesting mechanism than proven human outcomes.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Calcium alpha-ketoglutarate supplies alpha-ketoglutarate, a central Krebs-cycle metabolite with interest in energy metabolism, inflammatory tone, and aging-related signaling. It is one of the more popular healthy-aging compounds in longevity communities, but most of the confidence still comes from mechanistic and early-stage evidence rather than definitive human outcome data.

bioavailability.notes: Oral use is straightforward, but longevity claims depend far more on biological relevance than on simply swallowing enough compound.

half_life.notes: Practical use is guided by routine rather than acute pharmacokinetics.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals can improve comfort and routine adherence.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning placement is common, but there is no evidence that it is critical.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No standard cycling protocol exists, though ongoing reassessment is reasonable given the early evidence base.

known_interactions.description: Users should account for the calcium portion if stone risk, constipation, or hypercalcemia is relevant.

synergies.mechanism: Lifestyle inputs still matter more for healthy-aging outcomes than any single AKG supplement.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Calcium AKG can be part of an advanced healthy-aging stack, but it should not be treated like a validated longevity shortcut. Mechanism is ahead of proof.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Human healthy-aging evidence is promising but still early, with much of the excitement anchored in preclinical biology and small human datasets.

name: Carnosine
description: A dipeptide used for anti-glycation, antioxidant buffering, and in some cases muscle or cognitive support, with stronger mechanistic appeal than broad clinical proof.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Carnosine is a naturally occurring dipeptide that can buffer acidity and may help bind reactive carbonyl compounds involved in glycation and oxidative stress. It is therefore discussed for healthy aging, metabolic protection, and tissue resilience more than for a single narrow clinical endpoint.

bioavailability.notes: Oral carnosine is workable, but some degradation occurs. Even so, it remains a commonly used direct anti-glycation entry rather than relying only on beta-alanine loading.

half_life.notes: The mechanistic rationale often matters more than trying to optimize a short acute half-life.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is flexible; steady use is the more relevant variable.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: There is no established need to cycle carnosine.

synergies.mechanism: Both compounds are used in protocols focused on glycation stress and metabolic tissue protection.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Carnosine is conceptually attractive for healthy aging, but it should be viewed as a plausibly useful adjunct rather than a proven longevity driver.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Preclinical and mechanistic rationale is strong, with a smaller and more mixed human evidence base for long-term clinical outcomes.

name: Carotenoids - goji, carrots (Vit A, zeaxanthin, beta-carotene, lutein)
description: A whole-food carotenoid cluster used for eye, skin, and antioxidant support rather than as a single isolated longevity compound.

common_names: Carotenoids - goji, carrots (Vit A, zeaxanthin, beta-carotene, lutein)
ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This entry represents a carotenoid-rich food pattern or blend providing beta-carotene, lutein, zeaxanthin, and related compounds. These carotenoids are relevant for antioxidant defense, skin photoprotection, and especially visual support through macular and retinal protection pathways.

bioavailability.notes: Carotenoids are fat-soluble, so taking them with dietary fat improves absorption. Food matrix and preparation style also matter.

half_life.notes: Tissue accumulation and regular intake matter more than acute timing.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Absorption is better when taken with a meal containing some fat.
timing_recommendations.notes: This is nutrition-like support, so meal context matters more than clock timing.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Carotenoid support is generally best approached as an ongoing diet or supplement pattern rather than cycled.

known_interactions.description: High-dose beta-carotene supplementation has specific caution flags in smokers, which is different from normal food intake.

synergies.mechanism: Omega-3 complements carotenoid intake in visual and membrane-related support routines.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Carotenoid-rich foods are foundational and useful. The caution is mainly around isolated high-dose beta-carotene supplementation in the wrong population, not normal dietary carotenoids.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strongest for eye and photoprotection support from well-established carotenoids, while broader longevity claims are more indirect.

name: Carotenoids Complex
description: A blended carotenoid formula used for eye, skin, and antioxidant support, usually centered on lutein, zeaxanthin, and beta-carotene.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: A carotenoids complex provides fat-soluble pigments such as lutein, zeaxanthin, and beta-carotene that contribute to antioxidant defense, eye support, and skin photoprotection. It is best thought of as a concentrated nutrition-support blend rather than a singular therapeutic compound.

bioavailability.notes: Absorption is better with dietary fat. Formula balance matters more than pushing one isolated carotenoid excessively.

half_life.notes: Tissue accumulation matters more than acute timing.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Take with a meal that contains some fat for better absorption.
timing_recommendations.notes: Meal context matters more than exact clock timing.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Carotenoid support is usually continuous rather than cycled.

known_interactions.description: High-dose beta-carotene supplementation has specific caution flags in current or former smokers.

synergies.mechanism: Omega-3s complement carotenoid intake in eye and membrane-focused routines.

ai_profile.safety_notes: These blends are usually low risk and useful, but isolated high-dose beta-carotene is not automatically benign in every population.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strongest for visual and photoprotection support, with broader antioxidant and immune narratives being more indirect.

name: Cat's Claw (Uncaria Tomentosa)
description: An Amazonian botanical used for immune modulation, inflammatory balance, and in some preparations DNA-repair-oriented healthy-aging claims.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Cat's claw is a vine-derived botanical used primarily for immune and inflammatory support. Some branded forms such as AC-11 are specifically discussed for DNA-repair-related signaling, but that positioning is more specialized and less broadly established than standard immune-support use.

bioavailability.notes: Extract quality and whether the product is a general cat's claw extract or a branded AC-11 style preparation matter.

half_life.notes: Practical use is based more on daily routine than on precise half-life management.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals often improve tolerability.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is flexible.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Many users run immune botanicals in blocks or rotate them instead of keeping them indefinitely.

known_interactions.description: Because cat's claw may influence immune activity, caution is warranted with immune-suppressing therapies.

synergies.mechanism: Cat's claw can complement mushroom-based immune-support strategies through different botanical pathways.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Cat's claw is a reasonable immune botanical, but DNA-repair and longevity claims are more niche and less settled than general immune-support use.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is supportive but not definitive for immune and inflammatory use, with more specialized AC-11 claims resting on a smaller literature.

name: Cat's claw (urcaria tormentosa - AC11)
description: An AC-11-style cat's claw entry positioned more explicitly around cellular resilience and DNA-repair support than general herbal immune use.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: AC-11-style cat's claw preparations are marketed around cellular repair and DNA-support narratives alongside baseline immune modulation. The underlying botanical is still cat's claw, but the branded positioning leans more into healthy-aging and resilience than standard herbal immune support.

bioavailability.notes: The key issue is branded extract identity and quality rather than generic herb equivalence.

half_life.notes: There is no widely used PK model for consumer use.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals help with tolerability and routine adherence.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning use is common in resilience-focused stacks, but not mandatory.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Because this is used as a more experimental resilience compound, block-based use and reassessment make sense.

known_interactions.description: Immune-active botanicals should be used cautiously with immune-suppressing therapies.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is a more specialized branding of cat's claw, not a separate evidence class. The more ambitious DNA-repair claims should be interpreted carefully.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The evidence is intriguing but still niche, with a smaller literature and more branded-product dependence than more mainstream immune supplements.

name: Catalase (Aspergilus - Redenhair - Haarpigment komplex - foti+sawpalmetto)
description: A cosmetic-style catalase-centered hair pigment and antioxidant blend with limited evidence for systemic healthy-aging effects.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Catalase is an endogenous antioxidant enzyme that breaks down hydrogen peroxide. Consumer catalase blends marketed for hair pigmentation or cosmetic aging support usually combine catalase-related positioning with other ingredients, but convincing evidence that oral catalase blends reverse graying or materially alter aging biology is limited.

bioavailability.notes: Oral enzyme products often face basic questions about survival through digestion and whether intact systemic activity is meaningfully delivered.

half_life.notes: Human pharmacology for these cosmetic-style blends is not well defined.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals are a reasonable default for comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Any timing guidance is mostly conventional.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Because the evidence is weak, users should assess outcomes over a limited trial rather than assume indefinite value.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is a low-confidence cosmetic-support category. Claims around gray-hair reversal or systemic longevity should be treated very conservatively.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The rationale is mechanistically interesting, but modern clinical evidence for meaningful hair-pigment or longevity effects from oral catalase blends is weak.

name: Chaga
description: A medicinal mushroom used for immune support and antioxidant coverage, with a strong traditional reputation but a still-emerging modern clinical base.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Chaga is a medicinal mushroom rich in polysaccharides and antioxidant-associated compounds. It is generally used for immune support, stress resilience, and antioxidant coverage, but much of its reputation comes from traditional use and preclinical data rather than robust human trials.

bioavailability.notes: Hot-water or dual extracts are often preferred to simple raw powders. Product identity matters because mushroom supplements vary widely.

half_life.notes: This is best treated as a repeated food-botanical input rather than a single active molecule.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Tea or extract use can be taken with or without food depending on tolerance.
timing_recommendations.notes: Many users prefer daytime use as part of a tonic routine.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Users often rotate medicinal mushrooms rather than keeping every one in place continuously.

known_interactions.description: Extra caution is reasonable because mushroom extracts may overlap with bleeding-risk concerns in susceptible users.

synergies.mechanism: Astragalus and chaga are often paired in immune-resilience formulas.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Chaga is a reasonable medicinal mushroom, but it is easy to over-romanticize. Quality, oxalate concerns, and realistic expectations still matter.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Traditional use and preclinical literature are strong, while modern human supplementation evidence remains comparatively limited.

name: Chasteberry
description: Vitex agnus-castus, a hormone-focused botanical used mainly for menstrual-cycle support, PMS symptoms, and related prolactin-driven complaints.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Chasteberry appears to influence dopaminergic signaling and downstream prolactin regulation, which is why it is often used for PMS, cycle irregularity, breast tenderness, and some fertility-support contexts. It is a targeted hormone botanical rather than a general wellness herb.

bioavailability.notes: Standardized extracts are preferable for consistency because hormonal botanicals are hard to judge when products vary widely.

half_life.notes: Practical use is based on multi-week cycle support rather than acute kinetics.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food depending on tolerance.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning use is common in traditional vitex routines.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Vitex is often assessed over several menstrual cycles rather than treated as an indefinite supplement.

known_interactions.description: Because vitex appears to influence dopamine-prolactin signaling, caution is warranted with dopamine-active medications.
Hormone-focused herbs may not combine cleanly with every hormonal therapy strategy.

synergies.mechanism: Magnesium can complement vitex in cycle-related symptom support routines.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Chasteberry can be genuinely helpful in the right hormonal context, but it is not a casual beauty herb. It should be used with a clear cycle or prolactin-related rationale.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is reasonably supportive for PMS and some cycle-related complaints, though it is more targeted and context-dependent than a generic hormone tonic.

name: Chia
description: A fiber- and omega-3-rich seed used for bowel regularity, satiety, and modest cardiometabolic support as a whole-food supplement input.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Chia seeds provide soluble fiber, some protein, minerals, and plant omega-3 in the form of ALA. Their main practical value is improving stool quality, supporting fullness, and contributing to a more fiber-rich dietary pattern rather than acting as a potent source of marine omega-3.

bioavailability.notes: Fiber benefits depend more on hydration and consistent intake than on classic absorption. Ground or soaked chia may be easier to digest for some users.

half_life.notes: Benefits are driven by digestion, fiber fermentation, and nutrition pattern rather than plasma persistence.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Best taken mixed into yogurt, oats, smoothies, or water-rich foods. Fluid intake matters.
timing_recommendations.notes: Consistency and hydration matter more than timing.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Chia is best treated like an ongoing food-based support rather than a compound to cycle.

known_interactions.description: High-fiber foods and supplements can alter medication absorption if taken at the same time.

synergies.mechanism: Chia provides food-based fiber and nutrients while acacia offers a gentler supplemental soluble-fiber layer.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Chia is low risk and useful, but it works best as part of a high-fiber eating pattern rather than as a magical omega-3 substitute.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strongest for fiber-related benefits and satiety support, while omega-3 expectations should stay realistic because ALA conversion is limited.

name: Chlorella
description: A nutrient-dense green microalga used for broad micronutrient support and sometimes marketed for detoxification, though binding claims should be interpreted cautiously.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Chlorella is a green microalga that provides chlorophyll, carotenoids, minerals, and other nutrients. It is often used for general nutritional support and sometimes for heavy-metal or toxin binding narratives, but the strongest real-world role is still as a nutrient-dense algae supplement rather than a proven detox intervention.

bioavailability.notes: Broken-cell-wall products are typically preferred because intact cell walls can reduce digestibility. Product purity matters because algae quality varies.

half_life.notes: This is best treated as a nutrition-support input, not a single active drug-like compound.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals improve tolerability and fit its nutrient-support role.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is flexible; quality and tolerance matter more.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: It is usually used steadily like a greens-style nutritional adjunct rather than cycled.

known_interactions.description: Algae products may contribute vitamin K and should be used consistently and cautiously with vitamin-K-sensitive anticoagulation.

synergies.mechanism: Chlorella and spirulina are often paired for complementary algae-derived micronutrients, though this is not always necessary.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Chlorella is usually fine as a nutrient-dense adjunct, but detox claims are often stronger than the evidence. Product purity matters.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is supportive for nutritional and some biomarker effects, while heavy-metal and detox narratives remain more variable and product-dependent.

name: Chlorogenic Acid (Coffee Extract)
description: A polyphenol-rich green coffee or coffee extract used for glucose handling, appetite modulation, and metabolic-support routines.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Chlorogenic acids are coffee-derived polyphenols associated with effects on glucose absorption, hepatic glucose output, and broader metabolic signaling. In supplement form they are used mainly for weight-management and glucose-support narratives rather than as a primary stimulant, though some products also include caffeine.

bioavailability.notes: Standardization matters because some coffee extracts are mostly polyphenols while others include meaningful caffeine. The product identity changes the user experience significantly.

half_life.notes: If caffeine is present, stimulant effects can last longer and change timing decisions.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Often taken before or with meals when the target is glucose or appetite support.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing depends partly on whether the product includes caffeine.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No standard cycling is required, though users should reassess stimulant burden if caffeine is involved.

known_interactions.description: Coffee-polyphenol extracts may modestly overlap with glucose-lowering strategies and warrant monitoring in medication users.
Some extracts include caffeine and may not suit anxious or caffeine-sensitive users.

synergies.mechanism: Berberine and chlorogenic-acid-rich coffee extracts address glucose handling through partly different mechanisms.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is a reasonable metabolic adjunct, but product standardization matters. Many green-coffee products are less precise than their labels suggest.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is supportive but modest for metabolic and weight-management goals, with outcomes depending heavily on formulation and broader diet context.

name: Choline
description: An essential nutrient used for acetylcholine production, liver support, methylation, and cell-membrane maintenance.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Choline is required for acetylcholine synthesis, phospholipid membrane structure, and methylation-related metabolism through betaine pathways. It is foundational rather than exotic, and deficiency or under-consumption can matter for cognitive function, liver health, and pregnancy-related development.

bioavailability.notes: The experience depends heavily on the form. Plain choline salts differ from CDP-choline or alpha-GPC, which are more targeted nootropic forms.

half_life.notes: Plain choline and specialized nootropic forms have different pharmacology and use cases.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Food is a practical default, and some users prefer to emphasize eggs or other choline-rich foods first.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning use is common for cognition-oriented goals.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: As an essential nutrient, choline itself does not require cycling, though nootropic-style use may be adjusted based on response.

known_interactions.description: Some cardiometabolic discussions focus on TMAO generation, so total choline burden and diet context may matter in select users.

synergies.mechanism: Choline and DHA both support membrane structure and are often discussed together for brain and developmental health.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Choline is foundational, but more is not automatically better. Form selection and total intake matter.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strong for choline as an essential nutrient, while more aggressive nootropic or high-dose claims depend on context and chosen form.

name: Chondroitin
description: A cartilage-support supplement commonly paired with glucosamine for osteoarthritis and joint-comfort routines.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Chondroitin sulfate is a structural component of cartilage and connective tissue. Supplemental use is aimed at supporting joint cushioning, water retention within cartilage, and symptom relief in osteoarthritis-like joint discomfort, usually over longer-term use rather than acutely.

bioavailability.notes: Benefits are usually gradual. Product quality matters because cartilage-support supplements vary in purity and sourcing.

half_life.notes: Practical benefit depends on cumulative use over weeks to months.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals may improve comfort, though timing is flexible.
timing_recommendations.notes: Long-term consistency matters more than exact timing.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Joint-support compounds like chondroitin are usually run continuously during evaluation periods rather than cycled.

known_interactions.description: Some users and clinicians prefer caution because joint supplements can overlap with bleeding-risk discussions, though the signal is not dramatic.

synergies.mechanism: Chondroitin and glucosamine are often paired for structural joint support, though not every user responds meaningfully.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Chondroitin can be useful for some joint-health contexts, but it is not universal. Response is often modest and slow.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is mixed but reasonably supportive for some osteoarthritis-style symptom relief, especially with longer-term use.

name: Chromium
description: A trace mineral used for insulin-sensitivity and glucose-control support, especially in users with poor dietary status or insulin-resistance concerns.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Chromium is a trace mineral involved in insulin signaling and glucose metabolism. Supplemental chromium is used to support glucose control and sometimes appetite or weight-management strategies, though benefits tend to be modest and more likely when chromium status or metabolic control is suboptimal.

bioavailability.notes: Different chromium forms have different marketing narratives, but the real-world differences are often smaller than the branding suggests.

half_life.notes: Once-daily dosing is typically practical.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meal-linked dosing is common because the main goal is glucose handling.
timing_recommendations.notes: Taking it with meals is the simplest approach.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No standard cycling is needed, though ongoing need should be reassessed if there is no clear metabolic rationale.

known_interactions.description: Chromium may modestly add to glucose-lowering strategies, so monitoring is sensible in medication users.

synergies.mechanism: Chromium supports insulin signaling while berberine provides a stronger multi-pathway metabolic effect.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Chromium is usually safe at standard doses, but it is rarely transformative on its own. It is more useful as a small metabolic support tool than as a major driver of weight loss.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is modest and mixed, with the clearest use case being targeted metabolic support rather than broad consumer hype.

name: Chrysin
description: A flavonoid marketed for antioxidant and hormone-related benefits, but limited by poor oral bioavailability and a relatively weak human evidence base.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Chrysin is a flavonoid found in passionflower, honey, and propolis and is often discussed for antioxidant and aromatase-related hormonal effects. In practice, the gap between mechanistic interest and real-world oral efficacy is significant because bioavailability is low and human data are limited.

bioavailability.notes: Oral bioavailability is poor, which is the central limitation of chrysin as a supplement. Some products pair it with enhancers, but that does not solve the evidence problem cleanly.

half_life.notes: PK is less relevant than the fact that oral exposure is often underwhelming.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals are a reasonable default for tolerance, though they do not fundamentally solve poor bioavailability.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is secondary to formulation limitations.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Given the weak evidence and limited bioavailability, chrysin is better treated as an experiment than a permanent staple.

known_interactions.description: Because chrysin is sometimes used for aromatase or hormone-related reasons, caution is warranted in hormone-sensitive contexts.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Chrysin is mostly a mechanistic curiosity in supplement form. The main problem is not toxicity but overpromising despite weak practical evidence.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Preclinical rationale exists, but human efficacy data are thin and bioavailability remains a major obstacle.

name: Chyawanprash
description: A traditional Ayurvedic tonic paste used for vitality, resilience, and immune support, but formulation variability and sugar content matter.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Chyawanprash is a traditional multi-herb Ayurvedic formulation, often built around amla and numerous tonic botanicals. Its intended role is broad resilience, immune support, and vitality rather than a narrow single-pathway intervention, which also makes standardization and modern evidence interpretation more difficult.

bioavailability.notes: Because it is a complex multi-ingredient food-botanical matrix, the practical issue is formula quality and sugar content rather than classic bioavailability optimization.

half_life.notes: It is best thought of as a tonic formula, not a single isolated active ingredient.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Traditionally taken alone or with warm milk, though modern users may simply take it with breakfast.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is flexible and tradition-driven more than evidence-driven.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Traditional tonic formulas are usually used steadily rather than cycled, though modern users may run them seasonally.

synergies.mechanism: Amla is one of the central antioxidant-tonic ingredients in many chyawanprash preparations.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Chyawanprash is better framed as a traditional tonic food than as a precision longevity supplement. Sugar content and formulation quality are the main modern concerns.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Traditional rationale is strong, but modern standardized human evidence is modest and complicated by formula variability.

name: Cinnamon
description: A culinary spice used in supplement form mainly for glucose handling and post-meal metabolic support, with important sourcing differences between cassia and Ceylon.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Cinnamon contains polyphenols and other compounds associated with insulin-sensitivity and glucose-handling effects. It is most often used for post-meal glucose support and general metabolic routines, though potency and risk profile differ by species, especially because cassia cinnamon can contain substantial coumarin.

bioavailability.notes: Species matters. Ceylon is generally preferred for longer-term use because cassia carries more coumarin-related liver concern.

half_life.notes: Practical use is meal-linked rather than dependent on tight PK optimization.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Most relevant around meals, especially carbohydrate-containing meals.
timing_recommendations.notes: Meal timing matters more than time of day.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No standard cycling is required, but chronic high-dose cassia use is not ideal.

known_interactions.description: Cinnamon may modestly add to other glucose-lowering strategies.

synergies.mechanism: Chromium and cinnamon are often paired in consumer glucose-support formulas, though neither replaces larger metabolic interventions.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Cinnamon is practical and low risk when used intelligently, but cassia-vs-Ceylon distinction matters more than many labels admit.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is reasonably supportive for modest glucose-related benefits, especially when meal-linked and species quality is considered.

name: Cinnamon Extract
description: A more standardized cinnamon supplement entry used for insulin-sensitivity and glucose-support routines.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Cinnamon extract concentrates the glucose-support polyphenol story of cinnamon into a more standardized capsule form. It is used mainly to support insulin sensitivity and post-meal glucose handling, often with less bulk and less coumarin exposure than plain cassia powder.

bioavailability.notes: A standardized extract solves some consistency issues, but species, coumarin exposure, and product quality still matter.

half_life.notes: Use is typically driven by meal pattern rather than half-life.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Most relevant around meals where glucose control is the target.
timing_recommendations.notes: Meal-linked use is more logical than clock-based use.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No standard cycling is required.

known_interactions.description: Extracts may modestly add to blood sugar lowering and warrant monitoring in medication users.

synergies.mechanism: Berberine offers a stronger metabolic signal while cinnamon extract provides a gentler adjunctive layer.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Cinnamon extract is a cleaner way to use cinnamon for metabolic goals, but it is still an adjunct, not a major standalone intervention.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is supportive for modest glucose-related benefits, particularly in standardized products used around meals.

name: Cissus Quadrangularis
description: A traditional vine extract used for joint comfort, bone support, and sometimes body-composition routines.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Cissus quadrangularis is a traditional botanical used for musculoskeletal support, especially joint discomfort, connective-tissue resilience, and bone-healing interest. Some supplement positioning also extends into appetite or body-composition claims, though the main practical use remains joint and recovery support.

bioavailability.notes: Extract quality matters more than sophisticated PK concerns.

half_life.notes: Benefits are typically assessed over weeks, not acutely.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals are a practical default.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is flexible; consistency matters more.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Many users assess cissus over defined recovery or joint-support windows rather than taking it permanently.

synergies.mechanism: Cissus is often used alongside structural nutrition inputs in joint or injury-support routines.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Cissus is a plausible musculoskeletal-support botanical, but it is still supportive rather than curative. Extract quality and expectation management matter.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is promising but not definitive for joint and bone-support use, with traditional use outpacing robust modern clinical trials.

name: Cistanche
description: A traditional Chinese vitality herb used for energy, libido, and healthy-aging-oriented resilience, with interest in neuroendocrine and adaptogenic effects.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Cistanche is a traditional tonic herb associated with vitality, libido, stress resilience, and healthy aging. It is often discussed for adaptogenic and neuroendocrine effects, though the strongest modern claims remain ahead of the clinical evidence.

bioavailability.notes: Standardized extracts are preferable for consistency because whole-herb quality can vary.

half_life.notes: Its use is tonic-style and cumulative rather than acutely stimulant.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals are a practical default for comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning placement fits vitality-oriented use.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Users often run cistanche in vitality-focused blocks and then reassess.

known_interactions.description: Because cistanche is often used in hormone- and libido-oriented protocols, caution is reasonable in hormone-sensitive contexts.

synergies.mechanism: Both are often positioned for energy and libido, though they are not interchangeable and evidence remains mixed.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Cistanche is interesting and plausible as a vitality tonic, but longevity and testosterone claims are often stronger than the human evidence warrants.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Traditional use and preclinical data are compelling, while modern human evidence is still developing.

name: Coenzyme Q10 (Ubiquinol)
description: The reduced active form of CoQ10 used for mitochondrial energy support, cardiovascular health, and healthy-aging protocols.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Ubiquinol is the reduced form of coenzyme Q10 and plays a central role in mitochondrial electron transport and cellular energy production. It also contributes antioxidant protection in membranes and is widely used for fatigue, cardiovascular support, statin-associated depletion concerns, and general mitochondrial support.

bioavailability.notes: Ubiquinol is often marketed as more bioavailable than ubiquinone, and taking it with fat-containing meals improves absorption.

half_life.notes: Once-daily dosing is usually practical because tissue pools are relatively stable.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Absorption is better with a meal containing fat.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning or midday is common in energy-oriented routines.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: There is no established need to cycle ubiquinol.

known_interactions.description: CoQ10 has structural similarity to vitamin K and may complicate anticoagulation management in some contexts.

synergies.mechanism: Magnesium and CoQ10 both support energy metabolism through different but complementary roles.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Ubiquinol is one of the cleaner mitochondrial-support supplements, though premium pricing can exceed the practical difference over standard CoQ10 in some users.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strongest for mitochondrial and cardiovascular-support contexts, especially where CoQ10 status may be lower or energy demand is high.

name: Coenzyme Q10 (Ubiquinol) / MitoQ (Mitoquinol Mesylate)
description: A combined catalog entry covering standard ubiquinol and the more specialized mitochondria-targeted MitoQ longevity niche.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This entry combines standard ubiquinol with the more specialized MitoQ concept. Ubiquinol supports mitochondrial electron transport and antioxidant defense, while MitoQ is designed to concentrate a quinone-style antioxidant inside mitochondria more directly. The mainstream evidence base is stronger for ubiquinol than for MitoQ's broader longevity claims.

bioavailability.notes: Ubiquinol benefits from fat-containing meals, while MitoQ's differentiation is more about mitochondrial targeting chemistry than simple absorption.

half_life.notes: MitoQ pharmacology is more specialized, but real-world use is still generally once daily.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meal-linked use is practical, especially for ubiquinol absorption.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning or midday is standard for mitochondrial-support routines.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Neither ubiquinol nor MitoQ requires formal cycling, though cost-benefit reassessment is reasonable.

known_interactions.description: As with CoQ10, extra caution is sensible in anticoagulation-sensitive users.

synergies.mechanism: PQQ is often paired with CoQ10-style compounds in consumer mitochondrial formulas, though the incremental benefit is still debated.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This combined entry spans a relatively established compound and a more specialized mitochondria-targeted niche. The claims for MitoQ should stay more conservative than the ubiquinol baseline.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is solid enough for ubiquinol in mainstream mitochondrial support, while MitoQ remains more specialized and less broadly validated for long-term healthy-aging outcomes.

name: Coffee / Chlorogenic Acid / Caffeine
description: A combined coffee-performance-longevity entry centered on caffeine stimulation, chlorogenic acids, and broad metabolic signaling.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Coffee combines caffeine's alertness and performance effects with chlorogenic-acid-rich polyphenols that contribute metabolic and antioxidant signaling. In real-world use, it is one of the more practical and evidence-backed lifestyle compounds for alertness and possibly healthy aging patterns, though the benefits depend heavily on dose and tolerance.

bioavailability.notes: Brew method, roast, and tolerance all matter. Caffeine is reliably absorbed, while chlorogenic acid content varies by coffee type and preparation.

half_life.notes: Caffeine clearance varies significantly across users and can materially affect sleep.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food, though some users tolerate it better with breakfast.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing matters primarily because sleep disruption can erase many benefits.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Tolerance can build and sleep debt can accumulate, so periodic dose control is useful.

known_interactions.description: Caffeine can worsen anxiety, palpitations, or overstimulation in sensitive users.

synergies.mechanism: L-theanine can reduce some jitteriness while preserving useful alertness from caffeine.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Coffee is one of the most useful performance and healthy-aging-adjacent compounds available, but only when dose remains compatible with sleep and stress resilience.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strong for alertness and performance and reasonably supportive for long-term health patterns, provided intake is moderate and individually tolerated.

name: Collagen Peptides
description: Hydrolyzed collagen used for skin elasticity, tendon and ligament support, and broader connective-tissue nutrition.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Collagen peptides provide glycine-, proline-, and hydroxyproline-rich amino acids that support collagen-containing tissues such as skin, tendons, ligaments, and joint structures. Their use is nutritional and structural rather than pharmacologic, with best results usually seen over sustained use.

bioavailability.notes: Hydrolyzed collagen is designed to be easy to digest. Practical value depends more on dose consistency and total protein context than on clever delivery systems.

half_life.notes: Benefits are cumulative and tissue-oriented rather than driven by acute PK.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food. Some users pair it with vitamin C and tendon-loading sessions.
timing_recommendations.notes: Consistency matters more than strict timing, though pre-loading before rehab work is sometimes used.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Collagen is usually run continuously because it behaves more like a structural nutrition input than a cycling compound.

synergies.mechanism: Vitamin C is required for collagen formation and is often paired with collagen peptides in connective-tissue routines.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Collagen peptides are useful and low risk, but they should complement, not replace, adequate total protein and overall connective-tissue rehab strategy.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is reasonably supportive for skin and some connective-tissue outcomes, especially when used consistently over time.

name: Copper
description: An essential trace mineral needed for iron handling, mitochondrial enzymes, connective tissue, and pigment formation, but one that should be supplemented carefully.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Copper is an essential trace mineral involved in iron transport, energy metabolism, connective-tissue enzymes, antioxidant systems, and melanin formation. Supplement use is most defensible when intake is low, zinc intake is high, or there is a specific mineral-balance reason rather than generic wellness stacking.

bioavailability.notes: Copper is generally easy to absorb orally, but total intake and mineral balance matter more than the exact delivery form.

half_life.notes: Routine low-dose use is the practical model when supplementation is needed.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Taking it with food may improve comfort. It is often balanced relative to zinc intake.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is less important than overall mineral strategy.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: If copper is needed, it is usually taken steadily and conservatively rather than cycled.

known_interactions.description: High-dose zinc can reduce copper status over time, which is why these minerals are often considered together.

synergies.mechanism: Copper is often paired thoughtfully with zinc to avoid creating imbalance in either direction.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Copper is essential but easy to overuse unnecessarily. It should be treated as a precision mineral, not a casual add-on.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strong for copper's essential physiologic role, while supplement use should remain targeted to intake or balance needs.

name: Copper (Dark chocolate)
description: A food-based copper entry representing dark chocolate as a source of copper and related polyphenols, with dose moderation still important.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Dark chocolate contributes copper along with cocoa flavanols and a small stimulant load from theobromine and caffeine. It is best viewed as a nutrient-dense food input rather than a direct copper supplement, with cardiovascular and pleasure/adherence benefits that depend heavily on dose and product quality.

bioavailability.notes: Copper is obtained through the food matrix, which also brings cocoa flavanols, calories, and some stimulants. Quality and cocoa percentage matter.

half_life.notes: This is a food-pattern entry, not a purified supplement.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Consumed as food rather than as a discrete supplement.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is mainly about stimulant tolerance and calories.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: As a food source, it does not require cycling, but portion control matters.

known_interactions.description: Dark chocolate contains caffeine and theobromine, which may bother very sensitive users.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Dark chocolate is a good example of a food-first nutrient source. It is useful in moderation but should not be forced as a precision copper-delivery strategy if calories or stimulants are a problem.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is more about dark chocolate as a whole food than about copper supplementation specifically, but the food pattern can be sensible in moderation.

name: Copper + Zink (dissolved in water)
description: A combined trace-mineral entry intended to balance zinc with copper, though dissolved-water formats are usually less precise than capsule-based mineral dosing.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This entry represents a combined zinc-copper mineral strategy aimed at supporting immune function, enzymatic activity, and long-term trace-mineral balance. The real physiological rationale is sound, but the dissolved-in-water presentation can be imprecise compared with clearly dosed capsules or tablets.

bioavailability.notes: Mineral form matters more than the water presentation itself. Citrates and bisglycinates are often better tolerated than oxides.

half_life.notes: Daily consistency and dose accuracy matter more than timing or PK.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Taking minerals with food improves comfort for many users.
timing_recommendations.notes: Exact timing is secondary to tolerability and dose accuracy.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Trace mineral balancing is usually continuous when needed, not cycled.

known_interactions.description: The main issue is cumulative mineral imbalance, especially too much zinc relative to copper or vice versa.

ai_profile.safety_notes: The physiology of zinc-copper balance is real, but the delivery should be precise. Ad hoc dissolved-water mineral approaches are more error-prone than cleaner standardized dosing.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Mineral-balance logic is established, but the specific dissolved-water format is less standardized and less evidence-grounded than conventional supplement forms.

name: Copper Peptides (GHK-Cu)
description: A topical copper-peptide skin and hair entry centered on GHK-Cu for wound healing, collagen signaling, and cosmetic tissue support.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: GHK-Cu is a copper-binding tripeptide used topically to support skin repair, collagen-related signaling, cosmetic wound healing, and in some products hair-follicle support. Its relevance is primarily local and dermatologic rather than systemic longevity.

bioavailability.notes: The goal is local skin delivery, not systemic absorption. Formula stability and compatibility with the rest of the skincare routine matter.

half_life.notes: Practical benefit depends on repeated topical use rather than systemic PK.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Not applicable for topical use.
timing_recommendations.notes: Evening placement is common in skincare routines.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Topical copper peptides are usually used continuously if tolerated.

known_interactions.description: The main concern is skin irritation or barrier overload when stacking multiple aggressive actives.

synergies.mechanism: Barrier-supportive products can make copper-peptide routines easier to sustain.

ai_profile.safety_notes: GHK-Cu is promising as a topical cosmetic active, but it should not be misrepresented as a proven systemic longevity intervention.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is supportive for local skin-repair and cosmetic use, while broader systemic anti-aging claims remain much weaker.

name: Copper Peptides (microneedling - face serum)
description: A microneedling-adjacent topical copper peptide entry intended for post-procedure skin recovery and cosmetic support.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This entry represents a topical copper-peptide serum used around microneedling or cosmetic skin-rejuvenation routines. The intended effect is local skin recovery, collagen-support signaling, and improved cosmetic healing response rather than systemic longevity biology.

bioavailability.notes: The goal is skin-surface and local dermal exposure. Barrier status after microneedling makes formulation gentleness especially important.

half_life.notes: Use is guided by skin response and routine tolerance, not systemic PK.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Not applicable for topical use.
timing_recommendations.notes: Placement should respect skin-barrier recovery rather than just routine convenience.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: These products are often used around procedure windows rather than as permanent daily staples.

known_interactions.description: The main concern is provoking skin irritation or delaying barrier recovery by combining too many strong actives.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is a cosmetic recovery-support tool, not a systemic anti-aging supplement. Post-procedure timing and barrier respect matter more than ambition.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Topical copper-peptide rationale is plausible, but procedure-specific protocols are driven more by cosmetic practice than by large modern clinical datasets.

name: Cordyceps
description: A medicinal mushroom used for exercise tolerance, oxygen utilization, and perceived vitality support.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Cordyceps is a medicinal mushroom positioned for vitality, oxygen utilization, exercise tolerance, and stress resilience. It is often framed as a performance adaptogen rather than a stimulant, though the quality of evidence varies by species and extract type.

bioavailability.notes: Species identity and extract quality matter more than sophisticated dosing tactics.

half_life.notes: Benefits are usually assessed over routine use rather than acute pharmacokinetics.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be used with or without food depending on tolerance.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning or pre-training use fits its vitality-support positioning.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Many users rotate medicinal mushrooms or emphasize cordyceps during performance-focused periods.

known_interactions.description: As with other medicinal mushrooms, immune-context caution is reasonable in select users.

synergies.mechanism: Creatine supports ATP buffering while cordyceps contributes a more adaptogenic and endurance-oriented layer.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Cordyceps is plausible and useful for some users, but it should not be marketed as a guaranteed VO2 max booster. Product quality matters.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is promising for vitality and exercise-support narratives, though results are mixed and heavily formulation-dependent.

name: Creatine Monohydrate
description: The most established performance supplement for ATP regeneration, strength, lean mass support, and increasingly cognitive resilience.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Creatine monohydrate replenishes phosphocreatine stores, helping regenerate ATP during high-intensity effort. Beyond strength and lean-mass support, it also has growing relevance for brain energy buffering and cognitive resilience in sleep deprivation, aging, vegetarian diets, and other high-demand contexts.

bioavailability.notes: Creatine monohydrate is well studied and generally effective without fancy delivery systems. The main determinant is adherence.

half_life.notes: Tissue saturation matters more than single-dose PK.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food. Many users tie it to training or a meal simply for consistency.
timing_recommendations.notes: Consistency is far more important than exact timing.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Creatine does not need to be cycled.

synergies.mechanism: Training provides the demand signal while creatine improves phosphocreatine availability and adaptation support.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Creatine is one of the most reliable and defensible supplements available. Most controversy is outdated or based on poor context rather than real-world risk in healthy users.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is robust across performance and increasingly supportive for brain-related energy contexts, making creatine one of the highest-confidence entries in the catalog.

name: Cryptolepis sanguinolenta
description: A potent traditional antimicrobial botanical used in niche infection-focused protocols, with limited human data and a need for caution.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Cryptolepis is a West African botanical with traditional use for fever and infectious conditions. Modern supplement interest is centered on antimicrobial and infection-focused protocols rather than general wellness or longevity, and its use tends to be more niche and more aggressive than everyday immune-support herbs.

bioavailability.notes: Extract quality and botanical identity matter. This is not a herb where generic quality assumptions are safe.

half_life.notes: Use is usually protocol-driven rather than PK-driven.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals may improve tolerability.
timing_recommendations.notes: Often used in divided dosing when part of infection-oriented protocols.
