cycling_recommendations.rationale: No cycling is needed for ginger.

known_interactions.description: High-dose ginger is sometimes discussed alongside bleeding-risk considerations.

synergies.mechanism: Ginger complements other digestive botanicals depending on whether nausea, motility, or post-meal heaviness is the main issue.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Ginger is one of the more practical, honest botanical tools in the catalog. It is useful because it reliably does a few things well, not because it is magical.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strongest for nausea and digestive-support use, with additional support for mild anti-inflammatory effects.

name: Ginkgo Biloba
description: A cerebral-circulation and neuroprotection botanical used mainly for cognition, memory support, and vascular brain health.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Ginkgo biloba extract is used primarily for cognitive support through effects on cerebral blood flow, oxidative stress, and neurovascular resilience. It is one of the more established botanicals in the cognition-support category, though it is not an acute stimulant.

bioavailability.notes: Standardized extracts matter more than raw leaf powder. Quality and standardization to flavone glycosides and terpene lactones are important.

half_life.notes: Cognitive effects are generally assessed over repeated use rather than as a one-dose event.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Food is optional but can improve comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning or midday is the most practical default.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No standard cycling is required for ginkgo.

known_interactions.description: Ginkgo is commonly discussed in relation to bleeding-risk considerations and should be used cautiously in that context.

synergies.mechanism: Ginkgo can support circulation and neurovascular tone while bacopa leans more toward memory consolidation and calm cognition.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Ginkgo is more legitimate than many cognition botanicals, but standardization and medication context matter.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is meaningful for some cognitive and neurovascular-support use cases, especially with standardized extracts used over time.

name: Glucosamine (sulphate)
description: A cartilage-support compound used for joint comfort and osteoarthritis-style routines, with a long history but mixed effect size across users.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Glucosamine sulfate is used to support cartilage matrix turnover and joint comfort, especially in osteoarthritis-style use cases. It is best thought of as a slow joint-support compound rather than an acute anti-inflammatory agent.

bioavailability.notes: Glucosamine sulfate is generally the preferred evidence-backed form compared with some alternate salt forms.

half_life.notes: Benefits depend on cumulative use over weeks to months.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals can improve comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is flexible.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Joint-support compounds are usually evaluated over sustained continuous use.

known_interactions.description: Sourcing matters for users with shellfish sensitivity.

synergies.mechanism: Glucosamine and chondroitin are often paired for joint-support protocols.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Glucosamine can help some users, but it is not universal and works slowly when it works at all.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is mixed but reasonably supportive in some osteoarthritis-style contexts, particularly with glucosamine sulfate.

name: Glucosamine Sulphate
description: A standard glucosamine sulfate entry used for cartilage support, joint comfort, and longer-term osteoarthritis-style routines.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Glucosamine sulfate supports cartilage matrix biology and joint comfort, especially in longer-term joint-support protocols. It is best used patiently and realistically, because the benefit profile is gradual and not dramatic for every user.

bioavailability.notes: The sulfate form is usually the reference point in evidence discussions.

half_life.notes: The meaningful variable is duration of use, not acute PK.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals often improve tolerability.
timing_recommendations.notes: Any meal-linked routine is fine.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No standard cycling is required.

synergies.mechanism: The glucosamine-chondroitin pairing is one of the classic non-pharmaceutical joint-support strategies.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is a slow, structural-support style product, not an acute pain reliever.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence remains mixed but credible enough for some joint-support use cases, especially over longer use windows.

name: Glutamine
description: An amino acid used mainly for gut-barrier support, GI recovery, and occasionally high-stress or heavy-training recovery contexts.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Glutamine serves as a key fuel source for intestinal epithelial cells and is often used for gut-barrier support, GI recovery, and stress-heavy training or illness recovery contexts. It is more convincing for gut-related use than for generic muscle-building hype.

bioavailability.notes: Glutamine is easy to take orally. The question is more whether the user has the right use case than whether it is absorbed.

half_life.notes: Practical use often favors divided doses when higher total intake is used.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food depending on comfort and routine.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing depends on whether the main goal is GI support or recovery support.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No standard cycling is required.

synergies.mechanism: Glutamine can sit alongside other tissue-supportive nutrition strategies when the goal is GI recovery.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Glutamine is useful in the right context, especially gut-related use. It is less compelling as a generic muscle supplement for already well-fed users.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strongest for gut-barrier and specific recovery contexts, while broader claims are more variable.

name: Glutamine (in collagen)
description: A hybrid catalog row linking glutamine-style recovery support with collagen-rich connective-tissue or gut-repair narratives.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This entry appears to frame glutamine inside a collagen or recovery context, emphasizing gut repair, connective tissue support, and performance recovery rather than treating glutamine as a stand-alone amino acid only.

bioavailability.notes: The practical logic is about fitting glutamine into a broader recovery-nutrition pattern rather than a unique delivery system.

half_life.notes: Frequent practical intake matters more than a long PK profile.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be mixed into collagen or recovery drinks depending on the protocol.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing follows the recovery use case.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No formal cycling is needed.

synergies.mechanism: Collagen supports connective tissues while glutamine contributes gut and recovery support.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is best treated as a recovery-support row, not as a special longevity mechanism.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The use case is plausible in gut and recovery settings, though the catalog wording is broader than the evidence base.

name: Glutathione (Liposomal)
description: A higher-bioavailability glutathione format used for antioxidant, detox-support, and skin-brightening routines.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Glutathione is a central endogenous antioxidant involved in redox balance, detoxification pathways, and cellular defense. Liposomal formulations aim to improve practical oral delivery compared with plain glutathione powders or capsules.

bioavailability.notes: Liposomal delivery is used specifically because ordinary oral glutathione has historically raised absorption questions. Product quality matters.

half_life.notes: The goal is improved systemic exposure and antioxidant support, not acute timing optimization.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is a simple default for adherence and comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is flexible.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No formal cycling is required, though many users run it in targeted antioxidant or skin-support phases.

synergies.mechanism: NAC supports endogenous glutathione production while liposomal glutathione attempts to raise direct glutathione availability.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Liposomal glutathione is a reasonable premium-format antioxidant support tool, but some of the marketing still runs ahead of truly decisive human outcome evidence.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is supportive for improving glutathione status and some targeted outcomes, though long-term healthy-aging claims remain less settled.

name: Glutathione (liposomal, reduced?, acetyl?)
description: An umbrella glutathione-format row covering multiple higher-bioavailability glutathione variants rather than one single clean product form.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This row appears to group multiple glutathione delivery concepts, including liposomal, reduced, and acetyl-oriented formats. The common rationale is improved antioxidant and detoxification support compared with plain oral glutathione, but the exact pharmacology depends heavily on which format is actually meant.

bioavailability.notes: The entire point of this grouped row is that delivery format matters. Liposomal and acetyl-style positioning are attempts to improve practicality over plain oral glutathione.

half_life.notes: The exact kinetics depend on which glutathione format is actually being used.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is a practical default for adherence and comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is less important than choosing a clear product format.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: This row already leans toward pulsed use, and targeted antioxidant-support phases are a practical fit for ambiguous format entries.

synergies.mechanism: NAC supports endogenous glutathione production while formatted glutathione products try to raise direct availability.

ai_profile.safety_notes: The main issue here is ambiguity. This is better treated as a grouped glutathione-format category than as a single well-defined compound.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Some support exists for improved glutathione delivery in better formulations, but the mixed-format row is inherently less precise than a single defined product type.

name: Glycine
description: A simple amino acid used for sleep quality, calming support, collagen synthesis, and metabolic resilience.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Glycine is a simple amino acid with roles in neurotransmission, collagen structure, and glutathione biology. Supplemental glycine is most commonly used for sleep quality, calming support, and as a basic recovery or connective-tissue nutrition input.

bioavailability.notes: Glycine is straightforward to use orally and does not require a fancy delivery system.

half_life.notes: Use is typically tied to the evening sleep window rather than long accumulation.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food.
timing_recommendations.notes: Evening use is the most common practical default.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No cycling is required for glycine.

synergies.mechanism: Glycine pairs well with other bedtime support compounds without needing to be complicated.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Glycine is one of the cleaner and more honest sleep-support compounds: simple, cheap, and often practically useful.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is reasonably supportive for sleep-quality and temperature-related sleep support, with additional rationale for recovery and collagen-related use.

name: Glycine (L-glycine) included in Collagen Peptides
description: A glycine-centered row framed inside collagen-peptide and connective-tissue nutrition rather than as a pure stand-alone glycine supplement.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This row links glycine's sleep and calming effects with its structural role inside collagen peptides. The practical meaning is that some of glycine's benefits can overlap with collagen-based recovery or connective-tissue strategies rather than existing only as a stand-alone amino acid story.

bioavailability.notes: This is less about special delivery and more about how glycine shows up in real-world collagen or amino-acid intake patterns.

half_life.notes: Practical use is about the timing window and nutritional context.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be mixed into collagen or other recovery drinks.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing depends on whether sleep or tissue support is the main goal.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No cycling is needed.

synergies.mechanism: Collagen provides a broader amino-acid matrix while glycine captures one of its key functional components.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is best treated as a context row, not as a separate exotic compound.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The glycine-sleep rationale is reasonably supported, and the collagen connection is structurally plausible rather than speculative.

name: Grapefruit
description: A food-based citrus entry with some metabolic and mitochondrial interest, but defined in practice by its major drug-interaction burden.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Grapefruit provides vitamin C, citrus polyphenols, and food-based metabolic support signals, but its defining practical feature is inhibition of CYP3A4 and related transport pathways that can significantly alter medication exposure. That makes it less of a supplement and more of a food item with real pharmacology baggage.

bioavailability.notes: Its importance lies in both nutrient content and the interaction chemistry of compounds like furanocoumarins.

half_life.notes: The drug-interaction effect can persist beyond the immediate meal window.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: This is itself a food interaction issue rather than a food pairing issue.
timing_recommendations.notes: Medication context matters more than clock timing.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: As a food entry, it is not cycled, but it may need to be avoided entirely depending on medication use.

known_interactions.description: Grapefruit can meaningfully raise levels of certain medications and should be treated seriously.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Grapefruit is healthy food for many people, but it is not benign in the presence of the wrong medications.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Nutritional value is real, but the clinically important point is its well-known interaction burden.

name: Green Tea Extract (EGCG)
description: A catechin-rich extract used for antioxidant, metabolic, and healthy-aging support, with stronger thermogenic and AMPK-style relevance than plain green tea alone.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Green tea extract provides concentrated catechins, especially EGCG, which are used for antioxidant support, metabolic signaling, AMPK-related pathways, and mild thermogenic effect. It is one of the more serious polyphenol entries, but high-dose liver safety questions mean more is not always better.

bioavailability.notes: Concentrated extracts are more pharmacologically relevant than brewed tea. Fasted use can sometimes worsen tolerability and liver-risk concerns.

half_life.notes: Polyphenol signaling and any caffeine content both shape the user experience.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Food improves comfort and may reduce problems with higher-dose extracts.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning or early afternoon is practical, especially if the extract contains some caffeine.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Some users run concentrated catechin extracts in defined blocks because of liver-tolerance concerns and because it behaves more like a high-potency polyphenol than a basic nutrient.

known_interactions.description: High-dose EGCG extracts can be harder on the liver and may also carry caffeine-related issues depending on the product.

synergies.mechanism: Green tea catechins are often paired with caffeine for metabolic-support formulas, though tolerance matters.

ai_profile.safety_notes: EGCG is useful, but concentrated extract use deserves more respect than simply drinking green tea.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is supportive for metabolic and antioxidant use, with the main limitation being the dose-dependent safety and tolerability profile of concentrated extracts.

name: Green tea extract (Epigallocatechin gallate EGCG, Catechins)
description: A broader EGCG-and-catechin longevity row that overlaps with standard green tea extract but reflects the catalog’s more general polyphenol framing.

common_names: Green tea extract (Epigallocatechin gallate EGCG, Catechins)
ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This row captures green tea catechins broadly, especially EGCG, for healthy-aging, antioxidant, and metabolic-support narratives. It overlaps heavily with green tea extract but is framed more broadly around longevity and polyphenol biology.

bioavailability.notes: Bioavailability and tolerability vary significantly across users and products. Food-linked use is usually safer than aggressive fasted dosing.

half_life.notes: The practical issue is dose and liver tolerance, not precise PK optimization.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Food is recommended for comfort and safety.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning or early afternoon is the safest general default.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Concentrated catechin extracts are often better used deliberately rather than treated like harmless tea.

known_interactions.description: Higher-dose catechin extracts can stress the liver and may also aggravate stimulant sensitivity.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is a legitimate healthy-aging polyphenol category, but concentrated extracts deserve more caution than tea-drinking culture implies.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is supportive for antioxidant and metabolic applications, with the main caveat being safety at concentrated doses.

name: HGH
description: Human growth hormone is a prescription hormone therapy category, not a conventional supplement, with significant endocrine and safety implications.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Human growth hormone is a peptide hormone therapy that affects IGF-1 signaling, body composition, tissue turnover, and metabolic function. It is therapy-like and pharmacologically potent, not a supplement category in any ordinary sense.

bioavailability.notes: This is an injectable hormone therapy where absorption and dosing are fundamentally clinical issues rather than supplement-format issues.

half_life.notes: Downstream IGF-1 and metabolic effects extend beyond the parent hormone's short half-life.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Not applicable in the supplement sense.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing depends on the medical or protocol context.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Any HGH use should be protocol- and lab-driven rather than continuous casual self-use.

known_interactions.description: HGH can materially affect growth signaling, edema, insulin sensitivity, and other clinically meaningful systems.

ai_profile.safety_notes: HGH belongs in the medication or therapy bucket, not the supplement bucket. It should be treated with full endocrine caution.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The biology is well established, but anti-aging use is controversial and safety-sensitive outside clear medical indications.

name: HMB
description: A leucine-derived muscle-preservation compound used for recovery, sarcopenia support, and training-stress resilience.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: HMB is a leucine metabolite used mainly to reduce muscle protein breakdown and support recovery or muscle preservation. It is more relevant in high training stress, aging, injury, or catabolic contexts than as a flashy everyday ergogenic.

bioavailability.notes: Oral use is straightforward. The main question is whether the user has a use case where HMB adds more than adequate protein and training do.

half_life.notes: Divided dosing is sometimes used, but practical context matters more than PK.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Food is optional but acceptable.
timing_recommendations.notes: Training or recovery timing is practical, but total daily intake matters most.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: HMB is often most useful in defined recovery, aging-muscle, or intense training windows.

synergies.mechanism: Creatine supports high-energy phosphate buffering while HMB leans more toward preserving muscle tissue under stress.

ai_profile.safety_notes: HMB is plausible and fairly clean, but it is context-dependent and not as universally useful as creatine or protein sufficiency.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is most favorable for muscle preservation and recovery-oriented contexts, with more modest benefit in already well-fed younger athletes.

name: HMB (Beta-Hydroxy Beta-Methylbutyrate)
description: A more explicit HMB entry for muscle-protein preservation, recovery support, and sarcopenia-aware performance routines.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: HMB is a leucine-derived metabolite used to reduce muscle breakdown and support recovery, particularly during heavy training, aging, immobilization, or lower-anabolism states. It is best framed as a muscle-preservation tool, not a dramatic muscle-building shortcut.

bioavailability.notes: It is easy to use orally, and the real question is whether the training or recovery context justifies it.

half_life.notes: Total daily dose and context matter more than exact timing.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals are fine, and pairing with other recovery nutrition is practical.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is secondary to consistent use in the right context.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: HMB is often most useful during defined periods of higher muscle stress or preservation need.

synergies.mechanism: HMB cannot compensate for inadequate overall protein intake.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is a sensible context-specific muscle-support entry, but it is not in the same evidence class or broad usefulness class as creatine.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is supportive in some recovery and muscle-preservation contexts, with more modest benefit in general use.

name: Hawthorn
description: A cardiovascular botanical used for blood-pressure, endothelial, and mild cardiac-output support with a long traditional history.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Hawthorn is a traditional cardiovascular herb used for circulation, endothelial support, and mild cardiac-support applications. It is more plausible as a gentle cardiovascular adjunct than as a dramatic performance or anti-aging compound.

bioavailability.notes: Standardized extracts are preferable for consistent use, though traditional tea and tincture forms remain common.

half_life.notes: Benefits are usually gradual and supportive rather than acutely dramatic.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is a practical default.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is flexible.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No standard cycling pattern is required.

known_interactions.description: Hawthorn can overlap with blood-pressure or cardiac-medication goals and should be used carefully in those settings.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Hawthorn is a reasonable cardiovascular herb, but it belongs in the careful-adjunct category rather than the casual wellness category for users on cardiac medication.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Traditional use and some modern data support cardiovascular adjunct use, though effect size is moderate and medication context matters.

name: He Shou Wu (Fo-Ti)
description: A traditional Chinese vitality and hair-support herb with long folklore use but real liver-safety concerns in some preparations.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: He Shou Wu is a traditional Chinese herb associated with vitality, hair pigmentation, and liver- or kidney-tonic narratives. Modern use is limited by uncertainty around efficacy and nontrivial hepatotoxicity concerns reported with some preparations.

bioavailability.notes: Prepared versus raw forms are treated differently in traditional contexts, but the modern safety concern remains a central issue.

half_life.notes: Use is primarily tradition-driven rather than PK-driven.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals are a conservative default.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is less important than product identity and safety.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Given safety concerns, long indefinite use is harder to justify.

known_interactions.description: Fo-Ti has a real association with liver injury in some contexts and should be treated cautiously.

ai_profile.safety_notes: He Shou Wu has more romance than reliability. The hair and vitality folklore is not enough to ignore liver-safety concerns.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Traditional use is longstanding, but modern evidence is limited and safety concerns materially affect the risk-benefit equation.

name: Holy Basil (Tulsi)
description: An adaptogenic Ayurvedic herb used for stress resilience, cortisol-tone support, and calmer energy.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Tulsi is an Ayurvedic adaptogenic herb used for stress resilience, calmer energy, and cortisol-related support. It tends to sit in the middle ground between purely calming herbs and more energizing adaptogens.

bioavailability.notes: Tea, tincture, and extract use are all common. This is a pragmatic adaptogen rather than a bioavailability-dependent compound.

half_life.notes: Benefits are usually assessed through daily routine and felt stress response.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Tea or capsules can be used with or without food depending on preference.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning or afternoon is the simplest default.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Users often rotate adaptogens or run them more during stressful periods.

synergies.mechanism: Tulsi and ashwagandha are often paired for complementary stress-resilience support.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Tulsi is one of the cleaner adaptogenic herbs, but it is still best understood as a steady stress-support tool rather than an acute anxiolytic.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is promising for stress-related support, with traditional use providing a strong backdrop even as modern trial quality varies.

name: Horny Goat Weed
description: A libido- and hormone-oriented botanical used for sexual vitality, nitric-oxide support, and male-performance marketing, with limited high-quality human evidence.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Horny goat weed is a traditional botanical, often standardized for icariin, used for libido, sexual function, and vitality support. Its practical positioning is more like a targeted sexual-health botanical than a broad foundational supplement, with mechanistic interest around nitric oxide, endothelial tone, and hormone-related signaling.

bioavailability.notes: Standardization to icariin or well-characterized extract quality matters more than raw-herb branding.

half_life.notes: The more relevant issue is effect variability and cardiovascular sensitivity, not precise PK.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is a practical default for comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Daytime placement is usually simplest.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Targeted libido or vitality botanicals are often run in blocks rather than indefinitely.

known_interactions.description: Horny goat weed may overlap with vascular and sexual-function pathways, so stacking with cardiovascular or erectile-dysfunction medications deserves caution.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Horny goat weed is more of a targeted sexual-health herb than a general wellness supplement. Claims often exceed the human evidence.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Traditional rationale and mechanistic interest exist, but rigorous human evidence is limited and safety context matters.

name: Horsetail
description: A silica-rich traditional herb used for hair, nail, skin, and connective-tissue support, with mild diuretic activity and limited modern longevity evidence.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Horsetail is a traditional plant rich in silica and used for hair, nails, skin, and connective-tissue support. It also has mild diuretic properties, which is part of why it should be treated as a functional botanical rather than a pure beauty ingredient.

bioavailability.notes: This is a traditional botanical where extract quality and tolerability matter more than advanced delivery systems.

half_life.notes: Practical use is based on daily routine and tissue-support expectations.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is fine and may improve comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Daytime use is simplest because of the mild diuretic profile.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Many users run horsetail in defined hair/skin support windows rather than as a perpetual staple.

known_interactions.description: Mild diuretic action may be unwelcome in users already prone to dehydration or electrolyte imbalance.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Horsetail is a reasonable tissue-support botanical, but it should be used with realistic expectations and respect for its diuretic effects.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Traditional and structural rationale is plausible, while modern human evidence remains limited.

name: Houttuynia
description: A niche antimicrobial botanical used in some infection-focused protocols, especially in Lyme-oriented alternative herbal stacks.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Houttuynia is a traditional East Asian herb that has been adopted into some niche antimicrobial and Lyme-oriented protocols. Its use is more targeted and protocol-like than broad immune support, and modern human evidence remains sparse.

bioavailability.notes: The main issue is botanical quality and protocol context rather than supplement-style absorption optimization.

half_life.notes: Use is protocol-based more than PK-based.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Food may improve comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Divided dosing is common in targeted protocols.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: This is better treated as a protocol herb than a lifestyle supplement.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Houttuynia is a niche antimicrobial-support herb. It should not be generalized into a daily wellness supplement without a clear reason.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Traditional use and protocol lore are much stronger than modern high-quality human evidence.

name: Huperzine A
description: A potent acetylcholinesterase inhibitor used for memory and learning support, with a profile closer to a CNS-active nootropic than a simple nutrient.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Huperzine A is a cholinesterase-inhibiting alkaloid from club moss that increases acetylcholine signaling. It behaves more like a CNS-active nootropic compound than like a nutritional supplement, which is why dose sensitivity and cholinergic side effects matter.

bioavailability.notes: This is pharmacologically active at microgram dosing, so the main issue is careful dosing and avoiding overstacking with other cholinergic agents.

half_life.notes: Its relatively long half-life is one reason many users avoid aggressive repeated dosing.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Food is optional but may improve comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning use is the safest practical default because of its duration.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Because it is cholinergically active and long lasting, intermittent use is often more practical than continuous daily use.

known_interactions.description: Huperzine A should not be casually stacked with other acetylcholine-active compounds or cognitive medications.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Huperzine A is effective enough to deserve drug-like respect in dosing and stacking decisions.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is meaningful for cholinergic cognitive effects, but the same potency that makes it interesting also makes careless use more problematic.

name: Hyaluronic Acid
description: A hydration-support molecule used for skin plumpness, joint cushioning, and tissue water retention.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Hyaluronic acid is a glycosaminoglycan involved in extracellular-matrix hydration, joint lubrication, and skin water retention. Oral use is generally positioned for skin hydration and joint comfort rather than dramatic structural repair.

bioavailability.notes: Oral hyaluronic-acid products are used pragmatically for hydration-oriented outcomes rather than because of an especially elegant systemic delivery story.

half_life.notes: Benefits are assessed over repeated use, not acute timing.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals are fine and may improve adherence.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is flexible.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No formal cycling is needed.

synergies.mechanism: Collagen provides structural amino acids while hyaluronic acid targets hydration and cushioning aspects.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Hyaluronic acid is a clean, targeted hydration-support entry, but oral use should not be confused with injectable aesthetic procedures.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is supportive for skin hydration and some joint-comfort outcomes, with relatively low overall risk.

name: IM8
description: A broad-spectrum branded foundation formula with a large multi-ingredient matrix aimed at energy, digestion, and general wellness coverage.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: IM8 is a large multi-ingredient foundation formula combining vitamins, minerals, probiotics, CoQ10, antioxidants, and adaptogens. Its value is convenience and broad nutritional coverage rather than precision targeting of one pathway.

bioavailability.notes: A formula with this many ingredients is best thought of as a convenience stack rather than a tightly optimized delivery system for each constituent.

half_life.notes: There is no meaningful single half-life because the formula spans many categories of ingredients.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals may improve tolerance for complex formulas.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning placement is the simplest default for a broad foundation formula.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No formal cycling is required for a foundational nutrition blend.

known_interactions.description: The main risk is unnecessary overlap or duplicate ingredient load rather than a specific single-compound problem.

ai_profile.safety_notes: IM8 is a convenience-forward formula, not a precision longevity tool. Its biggest value is simplifying routines, and its biggest downside is complexity and overlap.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence for products like IM8 is mostly indirect from ingredients rather than from strong independent long-term trials on the branded formula itself.

name: Indole 3 Carbinol
description: A cruciferous-vegetable compound used for estrogen-metabolism support and hormone-related skin or hair protocols.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Indole-3-carbinol is a cruciferous-vegetable compound used mainly to support estrogen-metabolism patterns. It is a precursor-related compound to DIM and is better treated as a hormone-context tool than as a generic healthy-aging supplement.

bioavailability.notes: Bioavailability and conversion to downstream compounds vary across users, which is part of why response can be inconsistent.

half_life.notes: Its downstream biology and user-specific conversion matter more than a simple half-life number.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals improve comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning placement is common, but the endocrine context matters more than timing.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Hormone-support compounds are best reassessed over defined windows.

known_interactions.description: I3C affects hormone metabolism and deserves more care in endocrine-sensitive users.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Indole-3-carbinol is a targeted endocrine-support compound, not a casual vegetable-derived wellness extra.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Mechanistic rationale is plausible and widely used in practice, though outcomes are context-dependent and not universally predictable.

name: Indole-3-Carbinol
description: A duplicate I3C row for hormone-metabolism support and cruciferous-derived endocrine balance routines.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Indole-3-carbinol is a cruciferous-derived compound used to influence estrogen metabolism and is a precursor-related step toward compounds like DIM. It is best used when there is a real hormone-support rationale rather than as a generic antioxidant.

bioavailability.notes: Its biology depends on conversion patterns and user-specific response.

half_life.notes: Practical response matters more than exact PK.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals improve comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Consistency matters more than time of day.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: This is generally best run in reassessed hormonal-support windows.

known_interactions.description: I3C should be used carefully when hormones are already being manipulated pharmacologically.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is another endocrine-context entry, not a general wellness staple.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is biologically plausible and practically used, though the response profile is variable and context-sensitive.

name: Inulin
description: A prebiotic fiber used to feed beneficial gut microbes, improve bowel regularity, and support metabolic health through fermentation.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Inulin is a fermentable prebiotic fiber that selectively feeds beneficial gut microbes, especially bifidobacteria, and supports short-chain-fatty-acid production. It is useful but can be more gas-producing than gentler fibers like acacia.

bioavailability.notes: The key determinant is tolerance and fermentability, not systemic absorption.

half_life.notes: Its value comes from gut fermentation and microbiome effects.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Works well mixed into food or beverages, with fluids.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is secondary to tolerance and consistency.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Prebiotic fiber is typically used continuously when tolerated.

known_interactions.description: Fiber can alter medication absorption timing if taken too closely together.

synergies.mechanism: Inulin can help feed beneficial microbes while probiotics add them directly.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Inulin is effective prebiotic fiber, but many users do better if it is introduced carefully and not mistaken for a zero-side-effect ingredient.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is solid for prebiotic and microbiome support, with the main real-world limitation being GI tolerance.

name: Iodine
description: An essential thyroid-related trace mineral that should be treated as a precision nutrient rather than a casual immune booster.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Iodine is essential for thyroid hormone synthesis and downstream metabolic regulation. While deficiency matters, excess iodine can also create real thyroid problems, which makes it a precision nutrient rather than a generic wellness supplement.

bioavailability.notes: Iodine is easy to absorb. The challenge is dose appropriateness, not delivery technology.

half_life.notes: Practical use is governed by thyroid physiology rather than simple daily PK.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Food is a practical default but not essential.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing matters far less than total dose and thyroid context.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: If iodine is used, it is usually taken steadily at physiologic doses rather than cycled.

known_interactions.description: Iodine supplementation can worsen or complicate thyroid conditions if used carelessly.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Iodine is essential, but it is not forgiving. The right dose depends on context, and excess can do harm.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strong for iodine's essential role, but the real lesson is to treat it like a precision mineral, not a generic immune hack.

name: Iraltone
description: A branded hair-support formula likely aimed at deficiency-linked hair quality rather than a broad joint or longevity intervention.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Iraltone appears to be a branded hair-support formula positioned around micronutrients and cosmetic support rather than a single active ingredient. Its benefit is most likely to show up in deficiency-linked or suboptimal-nutrition contexts rather than through a unique pharmacologic mechanism.

bioavailability.notes: As a brand formula, the real issue is ingredient composition and whether the user actually needs what it contains.

half_life.notes: A brand formula spans multiple ingredients, so there is no meaningful single half-life.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals improve comfort and adherence.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning use is the simplest default for a multinutrient hair formula.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Cosmetic-support formulas are often assessed over several months and then reconsidered.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Iraltone should be treated as a branded cosmetic-support formula, not a unique longevity compound.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The likely value comes from basic nutritional support rather than strong brand-specific evidence.

name: Iron
description: An essential oxygen-transport and mitochondrial mineral that should only be supplemented deliberately, usually when deficiency or high need is present.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Iron is essential for hemoglobin, oxygen transport, and mitochondrial electron transport. Its role is fundamental, but supplementation is only appropriate when deficiency, heavy losses, or elevated need make it necessary, because excess iron can also be harmful.

bioavailability.notes: Form matters for tolerability, and absorption is influenced by food and competing minerals. The central question is whether the user should be taking iron at all.

half_life.notes: Iron supplementation is governed by repletion dynamics, not just daily PK.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Food can reduce GI upset but may also reduce absorption. Vitamin C can help absorption.
timing_recommendations.notes: The tolerability-vs-absorption tradeoff matters more than a generic timing rule.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Iron is often taken in repletion phases and then reassessed with labs rather than continued indefinitely.

known_interactions.description: Iron should not be used casually in iron-overload states and can interfere with absorption of some medications or minerals.

synergies.mechanism: Vitamin C can enhance non-heme iron absorption.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Iron is highly effective when truly needed and inappropriate when it is not. It belongs in the precision-mineral category, not the general supplement category.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strong for iron's essential role and for repletion when deficiency exists, with the main risk being overuse or poorly guided use.

name: Japanese Knotweed
description: A resveratrol-rich botanical used in both healthy-aging and niche antimicrobial herbal protocols, with more tradition and protocol lore than settled outcomes.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Japanese knotweed is a botanical best known in supplements as a resveratrol source, but it also appears in niche infection-oriented herbal protocols. It is better treated as a targeted botanical category than as a simple purified resveratrol equivalent.

bioavailability.notes: Its relevance depends on extract quality and whether the product is being used for general polyphenol support or a more targeted protocol context.

half_life.notes: The extract contains multiple relevant compounds, so there is no single defining half-life.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals are a practical default for comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Divided use is common when it appears in protocol-style stacks.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Many users employ it in defined anti-inflammatory or niche protocol windows rather than indefinitely.

known_interactions.description: Japanese knotweed and resveratrol-rich botanicals can overlap with bleeding-risk considerations.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Japanese knotweed is plausible, but it is more useful when tied to a clear objective than when used as a vague longevity decoration.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Mechanistic and botanical rationale is meaningful, while human outcome data remain more limited and context-specific.

name: Kaempferol
description: A flavonoid found in several plant foods with emerging anti-inflammatory and senolytic-interest signaling relevant to healthy-aging stacks.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Kaempferol is a dietary flavonoid found in greens, tea, and other plant foods, with interest in anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and possible senolytic or stress-response signaling. It fits the same broad family as other healthy-aging flavonoid compounds, though human protocol evidence is still early.

bioavailability.notes: Like many flavonoids, bioavailability is variable and the user experience is more about repeated signaling exposure than acute effects.

half_life.notes: Repeated intake patterns matter more than prolonged circulation.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is a simple default.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is flexible.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Some users treat senolytic-leaning flavonoids as periodic inputs rather than daily staples.

synergies.mechanism: Kaempferol and fisetin sit in overlapping healthy-aging polyphenol frameworks.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Kaempferol is interesting and relatively clean, but its strongest longevity claims still come from mechanistic and preclinical work.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The rationale is promising, though human intervention data remain early.

name: Kalium (citrate)
description: A potassium-citrate precision mineral entry more relevant to electrolyte balance, urinary chemistry, and cardiovascular stability than to generic longevity branding.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Potassium citrate provides potassium for electrolyte and membrane-potential balance and citrate for urinary chemistry relevance. It is more like a medical or precision-mineral tool than a casual supplement, especially in users with kidney, heart, or medication issues.

bioavailability.notes: Potassium is highly bioavailable, so the real question is dose appropriateness and safety, not absorption.

half_life.notes: Potassium handling is governed by renal and hormonal physiology rather than a supplement-style PK story.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Meals often improve GI comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Split dosing is often more practical than large single doses.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: If potassium is needed, it is usually used steadily and carefully rather than cycled.

known_interactions.description: Potassium supplementation can be dangerous in hyperkalemia-prone settings.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Potassium citrate is a precision tool, not a casual longevity mineral. Safety context comes first.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strong for potassium's physiologic importance and for potassium citrate in appropriate clinical or electrolyte contexts, with the main issue being misuse risk.

name: Keratin
description: A structural protein beauty-support entry used for hair and nail strength, though oral effect sizes are often modest and formulation-dependent.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Keratin is a structural protein important for hair and nails. Oral keratin supplements are marketed for cosmetic support, but the biology is less direct than many consumers assume because ingested proteins are digested rather than simply delivered intact to hair shafts.

bioavailability.notes: The main limitation is conceptual: oral keratin is not the same as directly restoring hair keratin.

half_life.notes: Outcomes depend on long-term cosmetic growth cycles, not acute PK.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Food is fine and may improve comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is mostly irrelevant.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Cosmetic-support products are usually assessed over hair and nail growth cycles.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Keratin is mostly a cosmetic-support category. It is low risk but should not be overhyped.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is modest and cosmetic-oriented, with benefits more likely to be incremental than dramatic.

name: Kos Greens Powders
description: A branded greens-powder family entry used for broad nutritional convenience rather than targeted mechanism-level intervention.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: KOS greens powders are broad nutrition-focused blends combining greens, plant powders, and sometimes digestive or probiotic-style ingredients. Their value is convenience and routine adherence rather than any one precision mechanism.

bioavailability.notes: A greens formula with many ingredients should be viewed as a convenience matrix rather than a high-precision delivery system for each ingredient.

half_life.notes: No meaningful single half-life exists for a blended greens formula.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Usually taken with water or in smoothies and meals.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning use is simplest for routine consistency.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No cycling is needed for a greens-style convenience formula.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is a convenience product category, not a special longevity technology. The main question is whether it helps the user sustain a better routine.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is indirect and ingredient-driven rather than based on strong independent trials of the branded formula.

name: L-Arginine
description: A nitric-oxide-support amino acid used for blood flow, exercise pump, endothelial function, and sexual-performance routines.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: L-arginine is a substrate for nitric oxide synthase and is used to support vasodilation, blood flow, endothelial function, and performance or sexual-health routines. It is effective in concept, though in practice citrulline often provides steadier nitric-oxide support.

bioavailability.notes: Arginine is orally usable, but first-pass metabolism is one reason citrulline is often preferred for raising arginine availability more reliably.

half_life.notes: Acute timing matters more here than it does for many general wellness compounds.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Some users take it away from meals for performance use, though food may help if the stomach is sensitive.
timing_recommendations.notes: Acute pre-workout or blood-flow timing is more relevant than all-day coverage.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No formal cycling is required, though some users reserve it for training or sexual-performance windows.

known_interactions.description: Arginine can overlap with vasodilatory or blood-pressure-lowering strategies.

synergies.mechanism: Beetroot targets the nitrate pathway while arginine targets nitric oxide synthase substrate availability.

ai_profile.safety_notes: L-arginine is legitimate for blood-flow support, but users should know it is not always the best nitric-oxide option compared with citrulline.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is supportive for blood-flow and endothelial-use contexts, though practical preference often shifts toward citrulline for similar goals.

name: L-Carnitine
description: A fatty-acid transport compound used for mitochondrial energy support, exercise recovery, and in some cases fertility or cognitive-adjacent protocols depending on form.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: L-carnitine helps shuttle long-chain fatty acids into mitochondria for beta-oxidation and energy metabolism. It is used for energy, recovery, and metabolic support, while certain forms like acetyl-L-carnitine are chosen more for cognitive or brain-related goals.

bioavailability.notes: Form matters because plain L-carnitine, acetyl-L-carnitine, and propionyl-L-carnitine are used somewhat differently.

half_life.notes: Tissue effects and form selection matter more than chasing precise timing.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is practical and may improve comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning or training-adjacent use is the most common default.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No formal cycling is required, though some users reserve higher dosing for targeted goals.

synergies.mechanism: Carnitine supports fatty-acid transport while CoQ10 supports electron-transport-chain function.

ai_profile.safety_notes: L-carnitine is useful and biologically plausible, but form selection and individual tolerance matter more than hype.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is supportive for targeted mitochondrial and performance-relevant use cases, with form-specific nuances shaping the best application.

name: L-Citrulline Malate
description: A performance-oriented nitric-oxide precursor used for blood flow, workout endurance, and pump-related training support.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Citrulline malate raises arginine availability more efficiently than oral arginine alone and supports nitric oxide production, blood flow, and exercise endurance. The malate component is often positioned as an additional exercise-support factor, though the main real-world value is still citrulline-driven nitric oxide support.

bioavailability.notes: Citrulline is often preferred over arginine for reliably increasing arginine availability and nitric-oxide support.

half_life.notes: It is primarily an acute performance-support ingredient.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Many users take it away from large meals for pre-workout use, though food is fine if needed for comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: This is a timing-dependent performance compound.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No formal cycling is needed, though many users simply reserve it for training days.

known_interactions.description: Citrulline can overlap with vasodilatory strategies and lower blood pressure in some users.

synergies.mechanism: Beetroot and citrulline target different parts of the nitric-oxide system and are often paired.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Citrulline malate is one of the more reliable blood-flow and workout-support compounds, with a cleaner practical case than arginine for many users.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is supportive for pre-workout blood-flow and endurance-style use, especially at adequate doses.

name: L-Lysine
description: An essential amino acid used for herpes-support protocols, collagen synthesis, and general protein sufficiency.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: L-lysine is an essential amino acid relevant for collagen formation and overall protein sufficiency. In supplementation, it is most often used for herpes-support protocols and occasionally for connective-tissue or skin-related nutrition support.

bioavailability.notes: Lysine is straightforward to use orally. The main issue is choosing it for the right goal, not absorption complexity.

half_life.notes: Targeted protocols often divide intake across the day.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be used with or without food depending on tolerance.
timing_recommendations.notes: Divided dosing is common in targeted use.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Many users reserve lysine for outbreak-prone or targeted support windows rather than treating it as a permanent daily staple.

synergies.mechanism: Lysine and vitamin C both fit naturally into connective-tissue support frameworks.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Lysine is a clean and practical amino acid, but it is most useful when tied to a clear herpes-support or connective-tissue rationale.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strongest for targeted herpes-support use and basic connective-tissue relevance, with relatively low overall risk.

name: L-Theanine
description: A calming green-tea amino acid used for relaxed focus, stress support, and smoothing stimulant edges.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: L-theanine is a tea-derived amino acid used to promote calm focus, reduce stress reactivity, and smooth the stimulating effects of caffeine. It is one of the more practical nootropic-adjacent compounds because the user experience is often clear and gentle.

bioavailability.notes: L-theanine is easy to use and does not require complex formulation tricks.

half_life.notes: The subjective calming window is usually the most relevant variable.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be used with or without food.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing depends on whether the goal is focus smoothing or evening calm.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No cycling is needed for theanine.

synergies.mechanism: The classic caffeine-theanine pairing is one of the most practical focus stacks because it improves subjective smoothness.

ai_profile.safety_notes: L-theanine is one of the cleaner and more useful calm-focus entries in the catalog.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is supportive for relaxed focus and stress support, especially in caffeine-paired use cases.

name: L-Tyrosine
description: A catecholamine-precursor amino acid used for focus and stress resilience when cognitive demand is high.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: L-tyrosine is a precursor to dopamine, norepinephrine, and epinephrine and is used mainly to support cognition and focus under acute stress or sleep deprivation. It is less about boosting baseline mood than about preserving performance when demand is high.

bioavailability.notes: Tyrosine is practical orally, but the best use case is acute stress-demand performance rather than chronic low-level use.

half_life.notes: It is generally used for acute or situational support.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Some users prefer it away from heavy protein meals for acute focus use, though food may be fine for tolerance.
timing_recommendations.notes: This is a task- or stress-timing compound more than an all-day tonic.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Tyrosine is often most useful when saved for high-demand periods rather than used mindlessly every day.

known_interactions.description: Catecholamine-related compounds deserve more care in medication-sensitive psychiatric settings.

synergies.mechanism: Caffeine and tyrosine are often paired for task-focused energy, though overstacking can backfire.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Tyrosine works best as a situational cognitive-support tool rather than a generic daily brain supplement.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is strongest for preserving performance under stress or sleep loss, rather than for broad everyday nootropic claims.

name: L-carnitine (+ Acetyl-L) 3 g/day
description: A grouped mitochondrial protocol row mixing L-carnitine and acetyl-L-carnitine concepts, likely alongside companion compounds rather than representing one single clean ingredient.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This row appears to group multiple mitochondrial-support ingredients around L-carnitine and acetyl-L-carnitine, and the imported note even references other compounds. It is best treated as a mixed protocol family row rather than as a precise single supplement entry.

bioavailability.notes: The key issue is that this row is not cleanly one ingredient. Form, companion ingredients, and gut metabolism all matter.

half_life.notes: This is too mixed a row for a single meaningful PK statement.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is a conservative default unless the specific protocol indicates otherwise.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning use matches the imported protocol-style wording.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Mixed mitochondrial stacks are usually run deliberately and reassessed, not kept as vague permanent routines.

synergies.mechanism: Carnitine-family compounds and CoQ10 are commonly paired in mitochondrial-support protocols.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This row is structurally ambiguous. It should be interpreted as a grouped protocol concept, not as a crisp single-ingredient entry.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Carnitine biology is plausible, but this specific grouped row is less precise than the cleaner standalone carnitine entry.

name: LKM5
description: A branded probiotic-style longevity entry that appears to refer to a specific microbiome formula or strain blend rather than a generic nutrient.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This row appears to represent a branded probiotic or microbiome-support formula positioned for longevity. The practical mechanism is likely gut-microbiome modulation, immune signaling, and metabolite production rather than a direct anti-aging pathway.

bioavailability.notes: For probiotic formulas, viability, storage, and strain identity matter more than marketing language.

half_life.notes: Probiotic impact depends on colonization dynamics and continued intake rather than simple plasma half-life.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is a conservative default for tolerance and adherence.
timing_recommendations.notes: Daily consistency is more important than exact clock timing.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Formula-style probiotics are often best assessed in defined blocks rather than assumed to deserve lifelong use.

synergies.mechanism: A probiotic formula generally performs better when paired with fiber and a diet that actually feeds the intended organisms.

ai_profile.sources_summary: The microbiome-longevity rationale is plausible, but branded formula evidence is only as strong as the exact strain blend and human data behind it.

name: Lactoferrin
description: An iron-binding milk glycoprotein used for immune defense, gut resilience, and targeted antimicrobial support.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Lactoferrin is an iron-binding glycoprotein that can limit microbial access to free iron while also influencing mucosal immunity and inflammatory signaling. It is most compelling in immune and gut-barrier support contexts rather than as a general wellness trophy ingredient.

bioavailability.notes: Protein integrity and formulation quality matter. Enteric protection can matter depending on the intended target.

half_life.notes: The practical question is mucosal exposure and immune effect, not a classic timing-sensitive PK profile.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Many users take it away from food, though exact timing is not the main determinant of benefit.
timing_recommendations.notes: Keep timing simple and consistent.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Lactoferrin is often most sensible when matched to an immune or mucosal-support reason rather than used indefinitely without a plan.

synergies.mechanism: Lactoferrin can complement microbiome support by shaping microbial ecology and mucosal defense.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is a legitimate immune-gut support ingredient, but it is still context-dependent rather than universally necessary.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is meaningful for some immune and GI applications, though the effect size depends on the use case and formulation.

name: Lactoferrin / colostrumamide
description: A grouped immune-gut row combining lactoferrin and colostrum-style concepts rather than representing one precise standalone ingredient.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This row appears to group lactoferrin with colostrum-style immune peptides or related dairy-derived bioactives. The shared logic is mucosal immune support, gut resilience, and recovery support, but the exact biology depends on which ingredient is actually being used.

bioavailability.notes: Because this is a grouped row, bioavailability depends on whether the product is a clean lactoferrin, a colostrum derivative, or a blended formula.

half_life.notes: This grouped entry is too ambiguous for one precise PK statement.

timing_recommendations.notes: Consistency matters more than perfect timing.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: This kind of grouped immune-gut support row is best run deliberately and reassessed.

synergies.mechanism: Dairy-derived immune bioactives often sit alongside probiotic or gut-barrier strategies rather than replacing them.

ai_profile.sources_summary: There is plausible support for mucosal and immune applications, but the grouped catalog row is less precise than a single defined ingredient.

name: Lactospore
description: A Bacillus coagulans probiotic entry positioned for reliable stomach-acid survival and practical gut support.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Lactospore is a spore-forming Bacillus coagulans probiotic designed to survive stomach acid and reach the gut more reliably than some fragile strains. It is best used for modest microbiome and digestive support rather than dramatic microbiome reinvention.

bioavailability.notes: The spore-forming design is the main delivery advantage here.

half_life.notes: Like other probiotics, effect depends on continued intake and ecological impact rather than plasma kinetics.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Defined trial periods help determine whether the probiotic is doing anything meaningful for the user.

synergies.mechanism: Microbial support usually works better when the diet provides substrate for the ecosystem.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is a sensible practical probiotic format, but it still deserves a defined trial rather than blind permanent stacking.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is decent for digestive-support use cases, especially because the spore-form format is practical and stable.

name: Lemon Balm

name: Lemon/lime juice
description: A food-based acidic citrus support entry used for digestion, vitamin C intake, and meal-context routines rather than as a true supplement.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Lemon or lime juice is fundamentally a food-based support input rather than a supplement. The practical value is mild digestive stimulation, vitamin C and flavonoid exposure, and use as part of meal routines or hydration habits.

bioavailability.notes: Absorption is not the issue here. The real considerations are dental enamel, reflux tolerance, and whether it meaningfully helps the user.

half_life.notes: This is a dietary habit input, not a pharmacokinetic supplement.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: This is itself a food-based input and works best as part of normal meals or diluted beverages.
timing_recommendations.notes: Dilution is a better optimization target than exact timing.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: This is a food habit, not something that requires formal cycling.

synergies.mechanism: Citrus flavoring can make hydration routines easier to sustain.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is a reasonable food-based support habit for some people, but it should not be mythologized into a high-leverage longevity intervention.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The value is mostly dietary and practical rather than tied to a strong supplement-style evidence base.

name: Lion's Mane
description: A medicinal mushroom used for cognitive support, neurotrophic signaling, and steady brain-health routines.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Lion's Mane is a medicinal mushroom positioned for cognitive and neurotrophic support, often discussed in relation to nerve growth factor signaling and long-horizon brain health. It is more plausible as a steady neuro-support tool than as an acute stimulant nootropic.

bioavailability.notes: Product quality matters more than hype. Fruiting-body identity and standardized extract quality are more important than generic mushroom branding.

half_life.notes: Meaningful use is about repeated intake over time rather than acute PK effects.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is a practical default.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning or midday works well for most users.

It is most often used for binding-style detox routines and galectin-3 or fibrosis-related discussions, though the clinical evidence remains narrower than the marketing.

bioavailability.notes: The entire point of modified citrus pectin is its processing. Product quality and particle profile matter.

half_life.notes: Practical use depends on regular intake and the intended binding or galectin-support context.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Away-from-food use is common when the goal is binding-style support.
timing_recommendations.notes: Spacing matters more than clock precision.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: This is a targeted intervention, not a baseline nutrient.

known_interactions.description: Binding-style products can potentially interfere with timing of other oral agents.

synergies.mechanism: Modified citrus pectin only really makes sense when there is a defined reason to use it.

ai_profile.safety_notes: The main issue here is not raw danger but using an expensive targeted tool without a real reason.
ai_profile.sources_summary: There is interesting but still limited evidence around heavy-metal and galectin-3-related use cases.

name: Molecular H2 in water
description: A delivery-format entry for hydrogen-rich water rather than a discrete standalone supplement compound.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This row is fundamentally about a delivery format: dissolved molecular hydrogen in water. The rationale centers on selective redox signaling and recovery support, but the key variable is whether the hydrogen is actually present at useful levels by the time it is consumed.

bioavailability.notes: The hardest part is not swallowing it. The hardest part is preserving dissolved hydrogen concentration long enough for it to matter.

half_life.notes: This is a transient exposure concept tied to the timing of actual consumption.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be used with or without food; freshness is more important.
timing_recommendations.notes: Consume soon after preparation.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Hydrogen-water products are easiest to justify as bounded experiments rather than permanent routines.

synergies.mechanism: Hydrogen water makes the most sense in recovery or oxidative-stress experiments with a real tracking plan.

ai_profile.safety_notes: The main problem is not toxicity but weak product reality and hype relative to the effect size.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Research is interesting but still mixed, and the delivery-format problem is central to whether the concept works at all.

name: Molecular Hydrogen (H2)
description: A molecular-hydrogen support entry used for selective redox and recovery experiments, with more promise than certainty.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Molecular hydrogen is used for selective redox and signaling support, with interest in recovery, inflammation, and healthy-aging applications. It is not a typical antioxidant in the broad scavenger sense; the appeal is more nuanced and the effect size is still being sorted out.

bioavailability.notes: Delivery method matters enormously. Hydrogen is hard to retain, so product design and freshness dominate practical effectiveness.

half_life.notes: Hydrogen exposure is transient, so protocol design matters more than nominal half-life.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Fresh use is more important than meal context.
timing_recommendations.notes: This is best treated as an experiment with timing linked to recovery or stress windows.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Hydrogen support is still emerging enough that bounded tracking-based use is more honest than indefinite stacking.

synergies.mechanism: The benefits of molecular hydrogen are subtle enough that actual tracking helps determine whether it is doing anything.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Hydrogen products are usually low-risk, but they are often oversold and under-validated.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The literature is promising but still not decisive, and practical effectiveness depends heavily on delivery quality.

name: Mucuna Pruriens
description: A dopamine-precursor botanical that behaves more like a neuroactive intervention than a normal stress supplement.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Mucuna pruriens contains L-DOPA, the direct precursor to dopamine. That makes it fundamentally more pharmacologic than most mood or stress botanicals, and it should be treated with the same caution you would bring to any dopamine-precursor intervention.

bioavailability.notes: The practical issue is extract standardization to L-DOPA content, not just the raw plant name.

half_life.notes: The active dopaminergic window matters more than any broad adaptogen framing.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Often taken away from protein-heavy meals when a more noticeable effect is desired, though tolerance varies.
timing_recommendations.notes: This is not a casual background supplement.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: A dopamine-precursor botanical is better treated as a bounded experiment than as an indefinite stack component.

known_interactions.description: Mucuna overlaps meaningful dopamine pharmacology and should not be mixed casually with dopamine-related medications.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Mucuna belongs in the medication-adjacent bucket because of its L-DOPA content, not in the same basket as gentle adaptogens.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The mechanism is real and the effect can be meaningful, but safety and neuropsychiatric context matter more than the supplement label suggests.

name: Multimineral and Antioxidant supplement
description: A broad-spectrum formula row that combines minerals and antioxidants but is too mixed to treat as one precise intervention.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This is a generic broad-spectrum formula row rather than a clean ingredient. The intended effect is nutrient repletion plus antioxidant support, but the real-world value depends entirely on what minerals, doses, and antioxidant compounds are actually inside the formula.

bioavailability.notes: Formula quality, dose balance, and mineral interactions dominate the real-world outcome.

half_life.notes: This is too mixed for one meaningful PK statement.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is the safest general default for mixed nutrient formulas.
timing_recommendations.notes: The main job is consistency and avoiding overlap.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Generic kitchen-sink formulas are best reassessed periodically rather than assumed to deserve indefinite use.

known_interactions.description: Overlap is the main problem with broad formulas, especially when users also take standalone minerals.

synergies.mechanism: A broad formula works best as a gap-filler, not as a substitute for basic dietary quality.

ai_profile.safety_notes: The main issue is not that the concept is wrong, but that vague all-in-one formulas often create overlap and false certainty.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Any evidence here depends on the exact formula rather than the generic category label.

name: Myc-P
description: An opaque product-style immune or antimicrobial row that appears to refer to a specific branded protocol formula rather than a known single compound.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This appears to be a branded or shorthand protocol product rather than a chemically precise ingredient. The catalog context suggests immune or antimicrobial targeting, but the real mechanism depends on the actual formula composition.

bioavailability.notes: Without a clear ingredient list, bioavailability discussion is necessarily limited.

half_life.notes: This row is too opaque for a meaningful single PK statement.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Opaque immune protocol products should not be treated as default forever supplements.

synergies.mechanism: The less transparent the product, the more important it is to tie use to a real protocol rationale.

ai_profile.sources_summary: Because the formula identity is unclear, the evidence quality here is necessarily weak and product-specific.

name: Myo-Inositol
description: An inositol isomer used for insulin signaling, ovarian support, and calming neuro-metabolic regulation.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Myo-inositol is a cell-signaling compound that supports insulin signaling, ovarian function, and mood or calming support in some contexts. It is one of the more practical metabolic-support tools when the use case is PCOS, insulin resistance, or related neuro-metabolic patterns.

bioavailability.notes: Myo-inositol is straightforward to take orally. The main issue is adherence to gram-level dosing.

half_life.notes: Repeated use is more important than acute timing.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food depending on comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Divided dosing works well for larger totals.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No standard cycling is required for inositol.

synergies.mechanism: Myo-inositol often works best when paired with metabolic lifestyle changes or a balanced inositol strategy.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Myo-inositol is one of the more useful and grounded metabolic-support compounds when the use case is right.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is especially good in insulin-signaling and PCOS-related contexts, with additional emerging mood-related support.

name: N-acetylcysteine (NAC)
description: A cysteine donor used for glutathione support, liver support, mucus regulation, and selected psychiatric or compulsive-behavior contexts.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: NAC provides cysteine for glutathione synthesis and is used for redox support, liver support, mucus regulation, and some neuropsychiatric applications. It is one of the more legitimate multi-context compounds in the supplement world, though it still needs a real reason to be in the stack.

bioavailability.notes: Oral bioavailability is not amazing, but the compound is still clinically meaningful because it reliably supports cysteine and glutathione biology.

half_life.notes: Repeated dosing can make sense in higher-targeted use cases.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food, though some users tolerate it better with light food.
timing_recommendations.notes: Divide higher doses for comfort.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: NAC is useful enough to justify ongoing use in some contexts, but it is still worth reassessing rather than leaving unexamined forever.

known_interactions.description: NAC can interact with nitroglycerin and related contexts.

synergies.mechanism: NAC fits well into a glutathione-support strategy when there is a clear oxidative or detox rationale.

ai_profile.safety_notes: NAC is one of the more honest workhorse compounds in the catalog, but it still works best when tied to a defined use case rather than vague longevity stacking.
ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is solid for glutathione and mucolytic biology, with meaningful support in liver and selected psychiatric-adjacent use cases.

description: A format/alias row for myo-inositol, the insulin-signaling and ovarian-support inositol isomer often mislabeled as a B vitamin.
