bioavailability.notes: This should be interpreted using ordinary myo-inositol logic. The main issue is adherence to gram-level dosing, not exotic delivery.

half_life.notes: The practical effect depends on repeated use.

synergies.mechanism: Inositol works best when the broader metabolic context supports it.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is a practical metabolic-support compound; the biggest risk is catalog confusion, not serious toxicity.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The evidence is inherited from the broader myo-inositol literature, especially in metabolic and PCOS-related contexts.

name: NAC (N-Acetyl Cysteine)
description: A cleaner NAC alias row for glutathione support, liver support, respiratory mucus management, and selected brain-health protocols.

half_life.notes: Repeated dosing can make sense when higher intake is used.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food depending on tolerance.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: NAC is useful enough for repeated use, but it still deserves periodic reassessment.

name: NDGA / Nordihydroguaiaretic acid
description: A niche antioxidant lignan with meaningful toxicology concerns that belongs more in experimental-compound territory than in routine supplement use.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: NDGA is a lignan with antioxidant and lipoxygenase-modulating activity, which is why it appears in some longevity and inflammation discussions. bioavailability.notes: Absorption is not the main issue. Toxicology and risk-reward judgment are the central issue.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is a conservative default for tolerance.
timing_recommendations.notes: This should not be treated as a baseline supplement.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: The toxicology profile makes indefinite use hard to justify.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This belongs in the experimental-compound bucket, not in routine supplement hygiene.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The mechanistic interest is overshadowed by toxicology concerns and weak practical evidence for safe wellness use.

name: NMN
description: A generic NMN row for NAD+ support and healthy-aging experiments, with strong enthusiasm but still incomplete long-term human outcome data.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: NMN is a precursor in the NAD+ salvage pathway and is used to support cellular energy metabolism and healthy-aging signaling. The mechanistic story is strong, but the human longevity outcome story is still much less settled than the hype suggests.

bioavailability.notes: Bioavailability and tissue delivery are still debated across formulations, which is why product quality matters.

half_life.notes: This is more about pathway support than about acute timing.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Morning use is a simple default.
timing_recommendations.notes: Most users take it earlier in the day.

ai_profile.safety_notes: NMN is one of the more interesting healthy-aging compounds, but the long-term benefit case remains more speculative than definitive.

name: NMN (Nicotinamide Mononucleotide)

bioavailability.notes: Product stability and delivery quality matter because the pathway discussion is more mature than the supplement quality landscape.

half_life.notes: Pathway support matters more than clock timing.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Morning use is the simplest default.
timing_recommendations.notes: Earlier-day use fits most users.

name: NR (Nicotinamide Riboside)
description: A clinically better-characterized NAD+ precursor than many competitors, though still stronger on biomarkers than on long-term outcome proof.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: NR is an NAD+ precursor used to support cellular energy metabolism and healthy-aging biology. Compared with many NAD+ supplements, it has relatively better human biomarker evidence for raising NAD levels, even though big hard-outcome longevity claims still outrun the evidence.

bioavailability.notes: NR has a stronger commercialization and clinical-validation story than many NAD competitors, though product quality still matters.

name: Nattokinase
description: A fibrinolytic enzyme from natto that behaves more like a clotting-management tool than a generic longevity supplement.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Nattokinase is a fibrinolytic enzyme derived from fermented soybeans and used to support healthy clot breakdown and circulation. Like lumbrokinase, it belongs closer to the anticoagulant-adjacent bucket than to ordinary wellness supplements.

bioavailability.notes: The main question is active enzyme quality and enteric survival rather than standard nutrient absorption.

half_life.notes: The important variable is coagulation context and bleeding risk.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Empty-stomach use is common for systemic enzyme products.
timing_recommendations.notes: This should be handled conservatively.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: A fibrinolytic enzyme does not belong on autopilot.

known_interactions.description: Nattokinase meaningfully overlaps with bleeding-risk territory.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is one of the clearest medication-adjacent entries in the catalog and should be treated that way.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The fibrinolytic rationale is real, but the safety context keeps this out of the casual-supplement category.

name: Niacin (Vitamin B3)
description: The flushing form of vitamin B3 used for NAD support and historically for lipid work, but one that becomes pharmacologic at higher doses.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Niacin is the flushing nicotinic-acid form of vitamin B3 and feeds NAD-related metabolism. At higher doses it becomes more pharmacologic, historically including lipid-related use, which is why it deserves more respect than a generic low-dose vitamin.

bioavailability.notes: Niacin is well absorbed; tolerability and liver safety at higher doses are the real issues.

half_life.notes: The practical issue is dose response and flush, not half-life optimization.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Food can improve comfort and soften flush intensity.
timing_recommendations.notes: Higher-dose use deserves more caution than basic vitamin framing implies.

synergies.mechanism: Niacin makes more sense when tied to a real goal rather than generic energy marketing.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Niacin is a real vitamin, but higher-dose use moves quickly into a more pharmacologic territory.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The vitamin role is unquestioned, while higher-dose performance and lipid-related use require more individualized judgment.

name: Nicotinamide
description: A non-flushing vitamin B3 form used for NAD support and sometimes skin-oriented support, with a cleaner feel than niacin but different tradeoffs.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Nicotinamide is the non-flushing amide form of vitamin B3 and supports NAD-related metabolism without the dramatic vasodilatory flush of niacin. It is useful as a cleaner B3 support option, though it does not behave identically to nicotinic acid.

bioavailability.notes: Absorption is straightforward. The main question is choosing the right B3 form for the intended use.

half_life.notes: Regular intake matters more than timing tricks.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Food is optional but often convenient.
timing_recommendations.notes: Morning use is a straightforward default.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No formal cycling is required at ordinary support doses.

synergies.mechanism: Nicotinamide works within the broader B-vitamin and energy-metabolism network.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is a reasonable B3 form for many users, but it is still best used with an actual nutrition or pathway rationale.

name: Nicotinamide (Vitamin B3 form)

name: Phenylethylamine

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Phenylethylamine is a trace amine with acute stimulant-like and mood-brightening effects, but it is rapidly broken down by MAO-B and therefore tends to have a very short-lived effect. It belongs closer to the pharmacologic nootropic bucket than to gentle daily mood support.

bioavailability.notes: The main issue is not absorption but rapid breakdown. Attempts to prolong it usually push the interaction risk up sharply.

name: Resistant Starch

bioavailability.notes: This is about microbial fermentation in the colon, not systemic absorption.

half_life.notes: The important effect is fermentation over time in the gut.

ai_profile.sources_summary: Evidence is meaningful for prebiotic and butyrate-support logic, with practical benefits depending on tolerance and context.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Tribulus terrestris is a traditional libido-support herb often marketed as a testosterone booster. In practice, the libido and vitality story is stronger than the case for major hormonal change.

bioavailability.notes: Extract standardization matters more than generic bulk-herb labels.

half_life.notes: The practical use is long-horizon and target-specific, not acute.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is a practical default.
timing_recommendations.notes: Earlier-day use is fine.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Tribulus is easiest to judge over defined libido or vitality windows.

synergies.mechanism: Tribulus does more as part of a functioning vitality routine than as a standalone promise.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Useful mostly for libido-oriented experimentation; the testosterone hype is usually overstated.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The libido-support evidence is mixed but plausible, while the testosterone-boosting reputation is much stronger than the data.

name: Tryptophan
description: An essential amino acid precursor to serotonin and melatonin used for sleep onset and mood-related support, but not free of interaction risk.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Tryptophan is an essential amino acid and precursor to serotonin and melatonin, making it relevant for sleep onset and some mood-support contexts. It is more biochemical and targeted than a gentle bedtime herb, which means medication context matters.

bioavailability.notes: The key issue is neurotransmitter context and competition with other amino acids, not weak absorption.

half_life.notes: The main use is tied to sleep or neurotransmitter support windows.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Often used away from protein-heavy meals when the sleep-support goal is emphasized.
timing_recommendations.notes: This is more targeted than a generic amino-acid supplement.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Sleep-targeted amino acids are easiest to judge over defined use windows.

known_interactions.description: Tryptophan overlaps serotonergic biology and should be used carefully in medication-sensitive contexts.

synergies.mechanism: Tryptophan often fits inside a more comprehensive sleep-support routine.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Useful when sleep onset is the target, but not something to combine casually with serotonergic strategies.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The precursor biology is solid, with practical value strongest for sleep-related use cases.

name: Turkesterone
description: A phytoecdysteroid performance compound with heavy marketing momentum and still-limited human evidence.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Turkesterone is a phytoecdysteroid marketed for performance and body-composition support. The mechanistic interest exists, but the human evidence is still too limited to treat it as a settled effective performance supplement.

bioavailability.notes: The biggest issue is weak human evidence and variable product quality rather than a clear bioavailability story.

half_life.notes: This is still more product-marketing-driven than clinically mature.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is a practical default.
timing_recommendations.notes: Treat this as an experiment, not a certainty.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Performance compounds with weak evidence are easiest to judge in training-specific blocks.

synergies.mechanism: Any potential benefit only makes sense inside a real hypertrophy-support environment.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Interesting but clearly still in the experimental performance-compound category.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The marketing is much more mature than the human evidence.

name: Urolithin A
description: A postbiotic mitophagy-support compound with one of the cleaner current mechanistic stories in the healthy-aging space.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Urolithin A is a postbiotic metabolite best known for supporting mitophagy and mitochondrial housekeeping. It has one of the more compelling current mechanistic stories in the healthy-aging space, though hard long-term outcomes remain less mature than the narrative.

bioavailability.notes: Direct urolithin A supplementation bypasses the variability of relying on gut conversion from ellagitannin-rich foods.

half_life.notes: The interest is repeated mitophagy support over time rather than acute effect.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is a practical default.
timing_recommendations.notes: Consistency matters more than timing tricks.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Tracked blocks remain the honest way to evaluate a still-emerging longevity compound.

synergies.mechanism: Mitophagy-support compounds are most coherent when the user is also challenging the mitochondrial system.

ai_profile.safety_notes: One of the more promising healthy-aging entries, though still better on mechanism than on definitive lifespan or hard-function outcomes.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The mitophagy and mitochondrial narrative is stronger than for many longevity compounds, while long-term outcome proof is still evolving.

name: Urolithin A / Pomegranate (Pomanox/Aurum/Mitopure)
description: A grouped urolithin/pomegranate family row mixing direct urolithin-A products with upstream ellagitannin or branded precursors.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This row groups direct urolithin-A products with pomegranate-derived precursors or branded variants. The unifying logic is mitochondrial housekeeping and ellagitannin-derived postbiotic support, but the exact strategy changes a lot depending on whether the product delivers direct urolithin A or relies on upstream conversion.

bioavailability.notes: Direct urolithin delivery is much more predictable than relying on gut conversion from pomegranate polyphenols.

half_life.notes: This grouped row mixes different delivery strategies, so one PK statement is not ideal.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is a simple default.
timing_recommendations.notes: Interpret as a family row, not a clean single ingredient.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: The grouped nature of the row makes tracked blocks more honest.

synergies.mechanism: Urolithin-family products are most coherent in users actually targeting mitochondrial function.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Useful family concept, but direct urolithin products and pomegranate precursors should not be treated as identical.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The mitophagy rationale is strongest for direct urolithin-A products, while precursor strategies are more variable.

name: Uva ursi
description: A urinary-tract herbal antimicrobial used for short targeted support only, not long-term daily wellness use.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Uva ursi is a traditional urinary-support herb used for short-term antimicrobial and urinary-tract applications. It is not a general immune tonic and should not be treated as a long-term daily supplement.

bioavailability.notes: The main issue is appropriate short-term use and urinary-context fit, not supplement-style optimization.

half_life.notes: The practical issue is safe short-term protocol use.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is a conservative default if it improves tolerance.

synergies.mechanism: Uva ursi only makes sense inside a coherent short-term urinary-support strategy.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is a short-term urinary-support herb, not a general longevity or daily-immune supplement.

name: Valerian
description: A classic sleep herb used for calming and sleep initiation, usually as a gentler botanical alternative to stronger sedatives.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Valerian is a classic sleep-support herb used for calming and sleep initiation, partly through GABA-related signaling. It is more sedative-leaning than many mild calming herbs, though still gentler than pharmaceutical sleep agents.

bioavailability.notes: Extract quality matters, though valerian is usually judged more by effect and tolerability than by elegant PK.

half_life.notes: The practical use is clearly bedtime-oriented.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food depending on comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Bedtime use is the obvious default.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Valerian is easiest to judge over defined sleep-support windows.

known_interactions.description: Valerian can add to sedation from other calming agents.

ai_profile.safety_notes: A reasonable botanical sleep aid, but still something to use deliberately rather than mixing indiscriminately with other sedatives.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The sleep-support evidence is mixed but serious enough to justify targeted use.

name: Vit D3/K3/A/E
description: A mixed fat-soluble vitamin formula row that combines several high-consequence vitamins and should be treated as a grouped precision-nutrition formula, not a casual general-health stack.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: This row combines multiple fat-soluble vitamins with distinct roles in immunity, calcium handling, epithelial health, and antioxidant defense. Because these vitamins accumulate and have meaningful toxicity or interaction ceilings, the row should be treated as a precision formula rather than a harmless kitchen-sink blend.

bioavailability.notes: Fat-soluble delivery and meal context matter, but the main issue is overlapping high-potency vitamins rather than weak absorption.

half_life.notes: Fat-soluble vitamins are about cumulative status, not acute timing.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: A fat-containing meal is the right context for this kind of formula.
timing_recommendations.notes: Precision and overlap control matter most.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Mixed high-consequence vitamin formulas should be used deliberately and reassessed.

known_interactions.description: This formula mixes vitamins with real toxicity and interaction ceilings, especially where vitamin K or E overlap with medication contexts.

synergies.mechanism: Fat-soluble vitamins are most useful when used with some real sense of need rather than broad optimism.

ai_profile.safety_notes: This is a precision formula, not a harmless all-purpose daily blend.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The vitamin biology is real, but the grouped formula deserves caution because multiple fat-soluble vitamins can accumulate or interact.

name: Vitamin A (Retinol)
description: A preformed vitamin-A row for vision, immunity, and epithelial integrity that carries real toxicity risk when pushed too high.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Preformed vitamin A is essential for vision, immune function, and epithelial differentiation. It is physiologically important, but unlike some water-soluble vitamins it becomes risky quickly when chronically overdosed.

bioavailability.notes: As a fat-soluble vitamin, meal context matters. The bigger issue is cumulative toxicity, not absorption.

half_life.notes: Vitamin A status is cumulative.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: Take with dietary fat for absorption.
timing_recommendations.notes: Precision matters more than timing.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: Vitamin A is useful but not a more-is-better nutrient.

synergies.mechanism: Vitamin A works inside a broader fat-soluble nutrient context and should not be over-isolated.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Useful but clearly a precision vitamin because chronic excess can cause harm.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The physiologic importance is unquestioned, while supplementation must stay conservative because of toxicity potential.

name: Vitamin C

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Vitamin C supports immune cell function, collagen synthesis, antioxidant defense, and iron handling. It is one of the more straightforward useful vitamins in the catalog, even if high-dose wellness mythology often runs ahead of the basics.

bioavailability.notes: Vitamin C is easy to take orally, though very high single doses are not necessarily better because absorption saturates.

half_life.notes: Dividing higher doses can make practical sense.

timing_recommendations.food_interactions: With-food use is fine if it improves comfort.
timing_recommendations.notes: Timing is flexible.

cycling_recommendations.rationale: No formal cycling is required.

synergies.mechanism: Vitamin C fits naturally into collagen and iron-related strategies.

ai_profile.safety_notes: Simple, useful, and often honest, but still easy to overhype at high doses.
ai_profile.sources_summary: The core physiology is clear and practical benefits are real, even if megadose claims are often overextended.

name: Vitamin D3
description: A fat-soluble prohormone-vitamin used for immune support, bone health, and mood-related resilience, but one that still deserves precision.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Vitamin D3 is a fat-soluble vitamin with prohormone-like effects across calcium handling, immune regulation, and mood-related physiology. It is useful and often necessary, but still belongs in a precision-nutrition framework because excess is possible.

bioavailability.notes: As a fat-soluble vitamin, meal context and overall status matter more than exotic delivery claims.

half_life.notes: Vitamin D status changes over weeks and months rather than acute daily windows.

name: Vitamin E (Mixed Tocopherols)
description: A mixed-tocopherol antioxidant vitamin used for membrane protection and broader lipid-phase oxidative defense.

ai_profile.mechanism_of_action: Vitamin E is a fat-soluble antioxidant that helps protect cell membranes from lipid peroxidation. Mixed tocopherol products are generally more coherent than alpha-tocopherol-only positioning, though high-dose antioxidant enthusiasm can still outrun the real benefit story.

bioavailability.notes: Fat-soluble meal context matters, but the more important question is whether the user actually needs extra vitamin E.

half_life.notes: Fat-soluble vitamin behavior is more cumulative than acute.
